metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_136", "source_md": "How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu\figure_8.jpg", "title": "Fig. 8. Contribution of different factors to the change of  PM_{2.5}  concentrations (a) and  PM_{2.5} -attributable premature deaths (b) in 2014 to 2020 ("Clean production") denotes process control measures, "Optimize fuel structure" denotes structure control measures, "Energy consumption control" denotes source control measures, "Co-effect of C and PM" denotes the co-effect of  CO_{2}  and  PM_{2.5} , "Death rate" denotes health care level).", "references": ["China implemented a stringent Air Clean Plan (ACP) since 2013 to address environmental and health risks caused by ambient fine particulate matter  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . However, the policy effectiveness of ACP and co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures to environment and health are still largely unknown. Using satellite-based  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  products produced in our previous study, concentration-response functions, and the logarithmic mean Divisia index (LMDI) method, we analyzed the spatiotemporal dynamics of premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, and quantitatively estimated the policy benefits of ACP and carbon mitigation measures. We found the annual  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in China decreased by  $33.65\%$ $(13.41\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  from 2014 to 2020, accompanied by a decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths of 0.23 million  $(95\%$  confidence interval (CI): 0.22-0.27), indicating the huge benefits of China ACP for human health and environment. However, there were still 1.12 million  $(95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by the exposure of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in mainland China in 2020. Among all ACP measures, clean production (contributed  $55.98\%$  and  $51.14\%$  to decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) and energy consumption control (contributed  $32.58\%$  and  $29.54\%$  to decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) made the largest contribution during the past seven years. Nevertheless, the environmental and health benefits of ACP are not fully synergistic in different regions, and the effectiveness of ACP measures reduced from 2018 to 2020. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  has become one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction since 2018, confirming the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Our study suggests, with the saturation of clean production and source control, more targeted region-specific strategies and synergistic air pollution-carbon mitigation measures are critical to achieving the WHO's Air Quality Guideline target and the UN's Sustainable Development Goal Target in China.", "The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure-related drivers (i.e., the drivers of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) avoided 0.10 million net premature deaths in the first phase (2014 to 2017) (Fig. 8b). The measures in ACP fully offset the increase in the number of premature deaths caused by the co-effect of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  as well as the population growth effect, indicating the explicit positive health effect of air quality improvements. The vulnerability factors (i.e., health care level and age structure) contributed to 0.03 million net premature deaths during 2014 to 2017. Amongst them, the improvement in health care level (decrease of baseline death rate) cannot fully offset the net deaths caused by the aging population in China, meaning that the health care level in China needs to be enhanced further. During the APWBSD, improvement in health care became a more effective driver than energy consumption control for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission to avoid  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable deaths, and can, thus, fully offset the effect of population aging. The population aging caused more premature deaths than economic growth, in the phase of APWBSD, which was distinct from APPCAP.", "We provide a comprehensive estimation of the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, as well as spatiotemporal changes in these, and a quantitative assessment of the effectiveness of ACP methods in mainland China during 2014 to 2020. The two key contributions are: (1) We estimated the spatiotemporal variation in the number of deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure in mainland China during ACP based on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  product with the finest spatial resolution available to date, and elucidated the spatial discrepancies in policy implementation across China. China's recent clean air actions have achieved great benefits for human health and the environment. However, over  $98\%$  of the population still lived under the risk of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution ( $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations over  $15\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), with 1.12 million ( $95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China in 2020, accounting for  $\sim 27.05\%$  of global ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. In addition, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic in spatial pattern, with the greatest health benefits detected in the YRD region and the best environment benefits identified in the BTH region. To achieve the co-benefits of environment and health, specific policies need to be adopted under different conditions. (2) We quantified the environment and health co-benefits of ACP measures and carbon mitigation from 2014 to 2020. Clean production was the most effective abatement measure in China ACP, reducing net  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and net premature deaths by  $18.35\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and 0.45 million, respectively. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  contributed to a  $23\%$  and  $26\%$  decrease in the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths and became one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase. This confirms the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of process and source control measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. With the exhaustion of end-of-pipe and energy consumption control measures, the future air clean actions should focus more on carbon abatement and energy structure optimization, especially under the background of global climate change mitigation. content.title: Fig. 2. Trends in national average precipitation pH and total  SO_2  emissions between 1995 and 2017. Precipitation pH data were obtained from 74 sites with continuous observations in the Acid Rain Monitoring Networks [10]; the blue belt indicates the interval within one standard deviation of the mean. Emission data for  SO_2  were derived from the MEIC model.
content.summary: The figure illustrates trends in national average precipitation pH and total SO2 emissions in China from 1995 to 2017, showing a correlation between pH values and emissions.
content.insight: There is a strong negative correlation between SO2 emissions and precipitation pH, with significant policy impact noted during the 11th Five-Year Plan, highlighting past and ongoing efforts to control pollution.

The trough of average precipitation pH values and the peak of total  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions both took place in 2006, when about one third of China's total territory area was at risk of acid rain. In terms of bulk sulfur and nitrogen deposition, the severity of the acid rain in China in 2006, when acid rain was at its peak, was estimated in the same range (if not higher) as that in central Europe in the 1980s [8].", "Although installation of FGD systems was required by the 2000 amendment of the Air Pollution Law, the implementation was not well enforced [20]. Consequently, the total  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions increased by nearly  $50\\%$  during 2002-2006, driven by a surge in coal consumption (Fig. 2), which worsened acid rain pollution nationwide [10]. This situation was improved during the 11th Five-Year Plan (FYP) (2006-2010), which set a concrete target to mitigate total  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions by  $10\\%$ . The 11th FYP was considered a milestone during which the air pollution control target became mandatory for provincial and municipal governments for the first time, enforced by the fact that local leaders could receive a sanction in cadre evaluation if they failed [21].", "Fig. 4 summarizes the major action plans for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  control implemented by the Chinese government since 2013. The policy framework shows a clear trend of gradually involving more categories of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  precursors and emission sectors, since traditional control actions for a single pollutant or a single sector were insufficient to deliver substantial reductions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations as required by the Action Plan. One notable case is that the estimated  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions and sulfate concentrations inversed from satellite observations both rebounded slightly during 2010-2011 due to a rapid increase in industrial activities and less stringent controls on the iron and steel industries (Fig. 2), which surpassed the reduction benefits of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  controls on power plants [31]. As a result, a series of stringent control measures have been implemented in the industrial polluting sector, including strengthening industrial emissions standards, phasing out small-scale high-polluting factories, phasing out outdated industrial capacities, and upgrading industrial boilers [32]. content.title: Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.
content.summary: The figure shows trends in PM2.5 and O3 concentrations between 2013 and 2017 in BTH and 74 key cities, highlighting a decrease in PM2.5 and an increase in O3 levels.
content.insight: Despite reductions in PM2.5, O3 pollution increased, posing challenges in simultaneous mitigation and highlighting the need for integrated pollution control strategies in China.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_180", "source_md": "Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its\\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.", "references": ["Despite the substantial progress that has been made in addressing acid rain and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  issues (Fig. 6(a)), an unwanted increase in ground-level  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution is still found in many regions across China, posing the more complex challenge of simultaneous continuous mitigation of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution with an emerging  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  issue. As  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution has been steadily increasing in most parts of eastern China [47-50],  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  issues have recently gained more attention among both the public and academia in China. As illustrated in Fig. 6(b), the average 90th percentiles in daily maximum  $8\\mathrm{h}$ $\\mathrm{O}_3$  concentrations increased by  $20.1\\%$  and  $24.5\\%$  in 74 key cities and BTH, respectively, from 2013 to 2017. Furthermore, densely populated regions of eastern China were exposed to high  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution, increasing the impacts of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  on human health. The results of the GBD project [51] estimated that respiratory mortality attributable to long-term  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  exposure in adults is between 274000 and 316000 in China, equal to approximately  $25\\% -29\\%$  of the deaths attributable to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure [16]. It is necessary to integrate  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  controls to ensure a pollution-free and healthy living environment in the development of ecological civilization in China.", "The biggest challenge of  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  pollution control is rooted in the complexity of atmospheric photochemistry formation, which depends on  $\\mathrm{O_3 - NO_x - VOC}$  sensitivity diagnosis and meteorological conditions [52,53]. A control strategy designed to reduce emissions of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  or VOCs for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  control may result in an unexpected disadvantage for  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  reduction. Strong VOC-limiting systems are found in most urban areas of China [54,55], and  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  in urban areas can be enhanced if few or no VOCs are controlled simultaneously with  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  [56]. For example, during the Action Plan from 2013 to 2017, anthropogenic emissions of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  were reduced by  $21\\%$  but VOCs increased by  $2\\%$  nationwide [57]. Such combinational changes in  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -VOC emissions might be beneficial for reducing  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  but not for  $\\mathrm{O_3}$ , as shown by the decreased  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and increased  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations during the same period (Fig. 6). It is notable that the increase in  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentration during the Action Plan period is also attributable to variation in meteorological conditions—specifically, changing temperature and short-wave radiation. Therefore, full consideration of meteorology and optimal mitigation of precursor emissions are critical in designing an effective  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  control strategy."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["PM2.5", "O3", "BTH region", "China National Environmental Monitoring Center", "90th percentiles"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows trends in PM2.5 and O3 concentrations between 2013 and 2017 in BTH and 74 key cities, highlighting a decrease in PM2.5 and an increase in O3 levels.", "inferred_insight": "Despite reductions in PM2.5, O3 pollution increased, posing challenges in simultaneous mitigation and highlighting the need for integrated pollution control strategies in China.", "paper_id": 26}

content.title: Fig. 2. Heating area and coal consumption (1997-2018).
content.summary: Fig. 2 depicts a timeline of policies related to air pollution control and energy transition from coal to cleaner alternatives in Northern China from 1963 to 2021.
content.insight: The figure illustrates the progressive implementation of domestic and foreign policies aimed at reducing coal consumption and improving air quality, culminating in the 'Winter Clean Heating Plan' for 2017-2021.

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_69", "source_md": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. Heating area and coal consumption (1997-2018).", "references": ["Due to the lower temperatures in Northern China, burning coal for heating had become the most common way in which residents kept warm in winter. Since the 1950s, the Government of China provided winter heating via coal-fired boilers in the north Huai River areas. Northern cities received free unlimited heating from November 15 to March 15. Up until now, heating has not only been an important indicator of social wealth but has also become necessary for the survival of northern people in winter. According to statistical data released by the Ministry of Housing and Urban-Rural Development (MOHURD) of the People's Republic of China, the annual growth rate of the urban centralized heating area reached  $14.04\%$  during 1991-2017 (Fig. 2). At the same time, coal consumption increased under the rising demand for heating in the two decades prior to 2013. The "coal-to-gas/electricity" policy has generally altered the previous energy consumption structure. However, the rural heating energy consumption structure has largely remained unbalanced, among which coal consumption accounts for approximately  $75\%$ . Moreover, the large scattered coal heating demand has caused a series of environmental and social problems. The combustion of coal in boilers is associated with the release of air pollutants, especially  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and total suspended particles (TSP). Winter heating has also reduced people's life expectancy in northern regions of China [27]. To meet the increasing demand for heating as well as to alleviate air pollutant emissions, the National Development and Reform Commission of the People's Republic of China announced its plan for the WCHP project (covering 2017-2021).", "implementing intervention measures have seen substantial air quality improvements [7,8]. For example, the United States of America has replaced coal with natural gas in generating electric power since 1997 and has achieved  $44\%$  and  $40\%$  reduction in  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emissions from the 1997 levels [9]. A cleaner heating technologies program in New Zealand has led to the significant decline of PM emissions and concentrations [10]. Such policies aiming to diversify energy structures with clean and renewable energy have been successful in certain countries, which also has important implications for China [11]. In Northern China, the annual consumption of coal used for heating is approximately 400 million metric tons, nearly  $50\%$  of which is separately distributed for use in rural areas. Moreover, the combustion of scattered coal heating has caused severe haze to occur in winter [12]. Chen et al. found that the combustion of scattered coal heating has led to an approximately two-thirds increase in air pollutants during periods when heating is employed (i.e., winter) compared to periods when heating is not employed (i.e., summer) [13]. Compared to coal, clean energies such as natural gas and wind power emit less  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ , and thus reduce human health risks from exposure to air pollutants [9]. Therefore, switching from scattered coal", "Studies on the effectiveness of energy transition have not produced a consensus among scholars. Lin and Zhu [18] found that fuel switching in "energy saving and emission reduction" (ESER) policy had failed to decrease  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations. Li and Chen [19] reported that a city that underwent coal-to-gas transition had no significant impact on reducing  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions, and this was primarily because the city focused on the replacement of industrial coal while ignoring the combustion of scattered coal heating in rural areas. Additionally, although Zhao and Luo [20] reported that a "coal-to-gas" project decreased pollutant emissions from a household sector by substituting civil urban coal consumption, they confirmed that this project had a limited effect on reducing industrial coal consumption. Liu et al. [21] adopted the GAINS model to examine the effect of coal switching strategy in Jing-Jin-Ji region, and estimated that the primary  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  decreased by  $28\%$  and  $11\%$ , respectively. Wu et al. [22] evaluated the household coal-replacement program in North China and concluded that coal-to-electricity policy achieved the goal of a clean winter, while the high-quality coal replacement policy had no improvement in indoor air quality. Since the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (smog) crisis in the winter of 2013, environmental governance in China has shifted attention to scattered coal heating in rural areas, after which a series of policies were implemented and subsequently achieved remarkable results [23]. However, official reports may have overestimated the benefits of the clean heating policy by simply attributing the decrease in air pollutant concentrations to the promotion of clean heating policies while ignoring the interference of other exogenous energy-saving and emission-reduction policies [24]. Additionally, policy impacts will vary in different regions; thus, it is necessary to identify the validity of such policy among regions. Few studies to date have focused on the evaluation of energy transition policies in China. As an important part of China's energy transition process, it must be determined that whether or not the "Winter Clean Heating Pilot" (WCHP) project improves air quality. Answering this question can offer some guidance in China's future environmental governance while also providing a quantitative reference for other coal-based, energy-consuming developing countries. It could also be beneficial to achieve the synergy between emission reduction and air quality improvement under the background of climate change.", "On the one hand, even though the WCHP project has proven to be an effective mean to control winter-derived heating emissions, the cost of this project should not be ignored. The conversion cost of "coal-to-gas/electricity" is higher and includes a subsequent increase in the cost of gas. Replacing unprocessed coal with clean coal can be used as a transitional method in the ultimate goal of energy transition for regions that experience considerable financial governmental pressure and low residential income. The government should focus on reforming the cost of electricity/gas by updating to more advanced heating technology and devices. On the other hand, since the reduction effect of the WCHP project on regional air quality varies under different development levels and resource distributions, project control should be delegated to regional governments based on local features rather than following the outdated pattern of "one-size-fits-all". {"id": "img_223", "source_md": "一种工业热源提取方法_利用温度特征模板的BP神经网络.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\一种工业热源提取方法_利用温度特征模板的BP神经网络\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig.4 Number of industrial heat source objects and industrial heat anomaly points in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas", "supplement": "(a) 工业热源对象 (b) 工业热源热异常点 图4 京津冀及周边地区工业热源对象和工业热异常点数量 ", "references": ["Abstract: Aiming at the problem of insufficient quantity and spatial refinement in the extraction of industrial heat source from annual scale thermal anomaly data, a neural network industrial heat source extraction method based on temperature feature template is proposed by using VIIRS active fire data. This study took Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas as the study area, Firstly, according to the spatial aggregation characteristics of industrial heat sources, the heat source objects were divided by the OPTICS algorithm. Secondly, according to the thermal radiation characteristics of the heat sources, the temperature characteristic template of industrial heat sources and non- industrial heat sources were constructed. Finally, the BP neural network was used to extract industrial heat source objects using the temperature feature template and heat source statistical characteristics as parameters. The results show that: (1) the extraction precision of industrial heat source of the neural network algorithm of temperature feature template proposed in this paper reached  $96.31\%$ . Compared with time filtering and logistic regression methods, the extraction precision of industrial heat sources was improved by  $8.45\%$  and  $7.53\%$ , respectively; (2) From 2015 to 2020, the number of industrial heat sources in the six provinces and cities in Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas decreased by  $27.46\%$ . The number of industrial heat source objects and heat anomalies in Hebei Province decreased by  $8.06\%$  and  $7.44\%$  annually, respectively, which was the largest decrease compared with other provinces and cities. The", "concentration of industrial heat sources in Shandong and Tianjin increased by  $25.72\%$  and  $86.64\%$ , respectively, indicating that the industrial transformation and upgrade policies in the two places have achieved remarkable results; (3) Tangshan, Handan, Lvliang, and Changzhi accounted for  $31.37\%$  of the total industrial heat sources in the study area, which are the main cities in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas. The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in seven cities such as Linfen and Taiyuan was higher than those in other cities; The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in 11 cities such as Beijing and Zhoukou was lower than those in other cities; (4) From January to May 2020, the number of industrial heat anomalies in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas remained unchanged or increased compared with the same period in 2019 and 2021. The COVID-19 had no significant impact on the industrial heat source in the study area. The number of industrial heat anomalies in Wuhan in January and February 2020 decreased by more than  $66.67\%$  compared with that in the same period in 2019 and 2021, the number of industrial heat anomalies from March to May 2020 was lower than that in the same period of 2019. The COVID-19 has had a significant impact on industrial heat sources in Wuhan from January to May 2020. This study reflects the current situation and trend of industrial heat source development in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas, which provides a valuable reference for the formulation and adjustment of relevant policies such as reducing energy consumption and improving secondary industry concentration.", "Key words: industrial heat source; OPTICS algorithm; temperature feature template; neural network; VIIRS Active Fire; Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas", "(d) Slope_NFHWH

content.title: Fig. 2. Comparisons of emission uncertainties of coal-fired power plants in Guizhou in 2005 using sector-based and unit-based methods. The “+” represents the central value and the box represents the  95\%  CI using the unit-based method, while the larger range line represents the  95\%  CI using the sector-based method.
content.summary: Figure 2 compares emissions uncertainties of coal-fired power plants in Guizhou, 2005, using sector-based and unit-based methods. Central values and 95% CIs are shown for each pollutant.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates that using a unit-based method reduces emission uncertainty compared to the sector-based approach, highlighting the importance of detailed source information.

content.title: Fig. 3. The analysis framework of the sources for energy increase in the passenger transport sector in China.
content.summary: The diagram outlines the sources of energy increase in China's passenger transport sector, detailing sub-sectors and influential factors like population and vehicle stock structure.
content.insight: The framework highlights the impact of policy measures on energy efficiency, emphasizing transportation structure adjustment and fuel economy improvements for significant energy savings.

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_74", "source_md": "Energy growth sources and future energy-saving pot.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Energy growth sources and future energy-saving pot\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig. 3. The analysis framework of the sources for energy increase in the passenger transport sector in China.", "references": ["analysis framework is proposed to consider different characteristics of traffic modes, as shown in Fig. 3. The effects of main driving factors on total passenger transport energy use are analyzed briefly. Furthermore, a specific investigation into three subsectors of passenger transport is performed in turn.", "# Energy growth sources and future energy-saving potentials in passenger transportation sector in China", "In China, energy use from passenger vehicles is increasing rapidly due to urbanization and income growth, but the contributions of transport service structure shift and energy efficiency improvement, especially future energy-saving directions are controversial and unclear because of the differences in the choices of traffic modes, which may greatly affect the analysis results and their interpretations. This study investigates the growth sources of energy use in the passenger transport sector during 1979-2017 and future energy-saving potentials by 2030 by re-examining statistical data. Results show that the transport service increase and transport structure shift led to the increase in energy use by  $665.25\%$  and  $337.22\%$ , but approximately  $14\%$  of the increased energy use was offset by energy efficiency improvements, and its effect is limited in public transportation sector, compared with private passenger vehicle sector. In public passenger transport sector, adjusting the service or volume structure may be a desirable option while in private passenger vehicle sector the policy focus is more on improving fuel efficiency and reducing average annual kilometers traveled per capita. Overall, there is still at least  $28.3\%$  of energy reduction potential by energy efficiency improvement before 2030 compared to 2017 baseline level. Potential evaluation showed that the current potential of transport service structure adjustment based on industrial planning is limited, which highlights the necessity of reform, involving promoting the sharing transportation, railway, subway and buses.", "In the last two decades, the transport sector has become a major source of energy consumption worldwide. In China, transport energy use increased from 58.63 Mtce (million tons of coal equivalent) in 1995 to 421.91 Mtce in 2017, with an annual average growth rate of nearly  $10\%$ . Energy use from passenger transport dominates total transport energy use, accounting for nearly  $60\%$  in 2017. By 2030, more than 300 million new rural residents are expected to enter the cities in China, which will have a great impact on the passenger transport energy consumption. Passengers are relatively easily guided by policy measures to change their energy-using behaviors in their daily travel, thus leading to low-carbon", "However, one of the key challenges is that the current energy statistics of passenger transportation are fragmented in China. The lack of comprehensive statistic specification causes that the data is scattered into different sectors in energy statistical system. The data does not properly cover metrics of interest for energy consumption analysis of passenger transportation. Part of the data is covered in the Transport, Storage and Post sector and Residential Consumption sector in China Energy Statistical Yearbook [3]. The former only includes the intercity public passenger transport and", "Many studies have investigated energy consumption and emissions increase in transport sector [2,5,6]. The relationship between transport energy use and socio-economic factors has also been intensively discussed using econometric models [7-11]. Various options to save energy and reduce emissions in the transport sector have been evaluated for their reduction potential, from both the technological and behavioral perspectives [1,12,13]. In transport studies, bottom-up methodology and scenario analysis were often used to study future trends, mitigation options, and reduction potential [14,15]. In particular, road transport and passenger transport have always been the hot topics for evaluating energy consumption and emissions [16,17].", "Either from an economy-wide or a sectoral perspective, the driving forces of increased energy consumption have been extensively investigated [18-20]. In the transportation sector, these studies could be summarized from two perspectives, namely, 1) the sectoral perspective, and 2) the vehicle-based perspective. From the sectoral perspective, changes in energy consumption and emission have been analyzed either for the whole transportation sector or its subsectors. Activity factors, namely Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth, population increase, and propensity to travel, were often found to be the main drivers of growing energy consumption [21-24], which is similar to the passenger transport subsector [25-27]. Other studies have focused more on micro sections of transportation. For example, on the vehicle level, the vehicle population, fuel mix, and vehicle fuel efficiency were", "With accelerating urbanization in China, passenger transport energy use has rapidly increased, from 28.78 Mtce in 1997 to 252.47 Mtce in 2017, with an annual average growth rate of  $11.47\%$  (Fig. 1). Energy use could thus be divided into three development stages. From 1978 to 1990, the intercity public passenger transport was the main traveling mode of passengers, as the urban and the private vehicle subsectors were undeveloped. From 1990 to 2002, energy consumption of the private passenger vehicles gradually caught up with that of the intercity subsector. From 2003 to 2017, the private subsector became the major source of the passenger transport energy use. The energy intensity of passenger transport decreased continually due to technological advances, and the energy intensity of the private passenger vehicles decreased rapidly from 143.68 Tce/Mp-km (ton coal equivalent/million passenger-kilometers) in 1997 to 51.38 Tce/Mp-km in 2017, due to the policy of fuel consumption limits for passenger cars [48]. This corresponds to a huge reduction in fuel consumption. Without this reduction in energy intensity, the fuel consumption in the private passenger transport sector would have increased nearly three times greater than it is today.", "(c) Modal energy intensity  
Fig. 7. Energy consumption and passenger volume share of three modes of the urban public passenger transport, 1978-2017. Data sources: China Statistical Yearbook [4]; China Urban Construction Statistical Yearbook [69]; Statistics bulletin of transportation sector development [64]; Fuel consumption limits for passenger cars, Fuel consumption evaluation methods and targets for passenger cars [48] and the authors' personal calculation.", "The data includes socio-economic activity, transport structure and energy intensity. The socio-economic activity data includes the population, the number of households and the GDP, which are from the China Statistical Yearbook [4]; the transport-related activity data includes the passenger service of the intercity public passenger transport, the passenger volume of the urban public passenger transport and the vehicle population of the private passenger vehicles, which are from 1) Passenger Kilometers of the Transport, Postal and Telecommunication Services, and Software Industry, 2) Basic Statistics on Public Transportation in Cities by Region of the Urban, Rural and Regional Development, 3) Possession of Private Vehicles of the Transport, Postal and Telecommunication Services, and Software Industry in the China Statistical Yearbook [4]. The structure data consists of the service structure of the intercity public modes, the volume structure of the urban public modes and the vehicle type structure of the private subsector, which are calculated based on the above transport-related activity data. The intensity data includes the energy intensity of the three subsectors and the average annual mileage of the private passenger vehicles, which are from the Yearbook of China Integrated transport [63], the Statistics bulletin of transportation sector development [64], the Railway Statistics [65], and the Fuel consumption limits for passenger cars [48]. The average annual mileage of the private passenger vehicles is from the survey data in the FEEI model [49].", "The GDP per capita effect was the dominant contributor to energy consumption growth and its contribution increased over time, but the contribution decreased during 1996-2000 (Figs. 5b) and 2008-2010 (Fig. 5c) due to the Asian financial crisis and the global financial crisis respectively. The shift of various modes of passenger transport (structure effect) contributed to the energy consumption decrease in the years leading up to 1990 because the passenger service share of civil aviation decreased (Fig. 5c). Since 1990, the structure effect turned from a negative value to a positive value. After 1990, the highway and civil aviation services were promoted vigorously by the State Council and Civil Aviation Administration of China [50,66]. Therefore, adjusting the transport structure in the intercity subsector, such as promoting the transfer form highway and civil aviation to railway, could be an effective way to save energy. The passenger service intensity effect provides an increasingly negative contribution to the increase in energy consumption during 1978-2017. The energy intensity of the railway, waterway, and civil aviation services decreased rapidly due to technological advancements and improved management.", "The GDP per capita effect was the dominant contributor to energy consumption growth due to rapid economic development in China. The most prominent contribution appeared during 1991-1995 and 2006-2010, with an energy consumption increase of  $275\%$  and  $195\%$ , respectively (Fig. 6b). The passenger volume structure effect was another main driver of the energy consumption growth due to the shift in the dominant mode of transport. From the late 1980s to the 1990s, the taxi industry grew rapidly, owing to the policy of open business implemented by the State Council", "The multiplication decomposition results in the three subsectors are shown in Fig. 9. Five influential factors on energy consumption can be observed and are quite similar in their effects in the intercity and urban passenger transport subsectors. The GDP per capita increase was always the dominant contributor to rises in energy consumption, but this effect was larger in the urban subsector (2.713). The passenger volume/service per GDP played a critical role in reducing energy consumption as opposed to energy intensity in the public transportation sector. In contrast, however, the private transport subsector showed that fuel efficiency improvement, decline of family size and average annual kilometers per capita offset the effect of vehicle stock growth per capita together.", "This study contributes to understanding the underlying driving factors of energy growth and exploring energy efficiency improvement opportunities in passenger transport sector in China. First, a comprehensive picture of passenger transport energy consumption trends during 1979-2017 in China is given. Main drivers of energy growth are briefly identified through re-classifying the fragmented data statistics, which, to our knowledge, are unique and original. Second, the effects of the main driving forces of increased energy consumption are investigated firstly by using a hybrid decomposition method. Third, the future energy-saving potentials of different policy pathways until 2020 and 2030 are estimated and compared involving sharing transportation, structure adjustment, energy efficiency improvement, and urban car ownership control.", "The results show that there are diversified contributors to energy consumption depending on the traffic modes utilized. On the whole, GDP growth per capita, motorization level and shifts in service structure mainly contribute to energy consumption increase in passenger transport sector during the period 1979-2017, and they cause more than 10 times increase in energy consumption, but the actual energy consumption increased just by 8.77 times because of the effect of energy efficiency improvement. For the intercity and urban public passenger transport, the GDP growth per capita, the service or volume structure change, and population growth strongly dominate the changes in energy consumption, and the effect of energy efficiency improvement is not very significant on a relative basis. In the private passenger vehicle sector, however, the situation is significantly different. Fuel efficiency improvement, decline of family size and average annual kilometers traveled per capita together offset the positive impacts from motorization level rise and vehicle type structure change. Especially, the fuel efficiency improvement has enormously contributed to energy conservation since 2010 in China.", "Outlook analysis shows that by 2030 energy conservation potential in intercity public passenger transport sector is limited (16.6%), but there are still huge energy-saving potentials in urban public passenger transport sector (54.3%) and private passenger transport sector (34.3%). They could totally lead to 31.6% of energy consumption reduction in passenger transport sector by 2030.", "In order to reduce energy use in passenger transport sector, multiple policy directions deserve attention. In China, the motorization level could be regulated by some mandatory policies, such as the vehicle license lottery in major cities, such as Beijing and Tianjin. In future, it is essential to give priority to public transport and sharing transport, such as the subway, bus, car-sharing, and bike-sharing. Particularly, first, sharing transportation deserves more attention in China because Chinese people are more likely to accept sharing and collective ideas, and there are less regulatory restrictions in China. Second, the energy intensity reduction targets up until 2020 need to be strictly enforced, especially for fuel efficiency standards for private passenger vehicles. Third, low-intensity transport modes, such as railway, waterway, rail transit, and mini vehicles, are strongly recommended to promote the low-carbon transition.", "[1] Waisman HD, Guivarch C, Lecocq F. Simulation domain.", "references": ["MEGAN v2.1 (Guenther et al. 2012) was utilized to estimate BVOC emissions in China with a  $27\mathrm{-km}$  horizontal grid spacing domain (shown in Fig. 1). The inputs for MEGAN include the LAI, PFTs, emission factors, and meteorological data (e.g., solar radiation, temperature, relative humidity and soil moisture). The monthly average LAI was obtained from the 8-day MODIS LAI product (MOD15A2, 2017) with the same horizontal resolution as the study domain, as shown in Fig. 2. The PFT map was obtained from the MODIS MCD12Q1 product and regridded into the WRF domain to compute the fraction of each PFT in each grid (Fig. 3). The default MEGAN emission factors  $(\mathrm{mg~m}^{-2}\mathrm{h}^{-1})$  with a resolution of  $30\mathrm{s}$ $(< 1\mathrm{km})$ , which reflect the BVOC emission rate under standard canopy conditions, were leveraged (http://lar.wsu.edu/megan/guides.html). Meteorological conditions were simulated by the WRF model. A one-year MEGAN simulation was performed for 2017 in China.", "We set up Community Multiscale Air Quality model (CMAQ version 5.2.1, Foroutan et al. (2017)) (https://www.cmascenter.org/cmaq/) to simulate the atmospheric composition over China with the same domain and grid resolution for WRF and MEGAN. The initial and boundary conditions for CMAQ simulation were based on the CMAQ default profiles which represent unpolluted atmosphere. The CB05 represented the gas phase, and AERO6 represented the aerosol chemical mechanisms (Appel et al. 2013). For the simulation of SOA, we used the default SOA module of the CMAQv5.2.1. It simulates NMVOC-derived SOA with a two-product model, treats primary organic aerosol (POA) as nonvolatile and nonreactive, and ignores IVOC emissions. Carlton et al. (2010) provided a detailed description of aerosol chemistry in CMAQ, including SOA formation from benzene, isoprene and sesquiterpenes. The POA oxidation mechanism is described in Simon and Bhave (2012). The new updates in CMAQ v5.2.1 accounts for the semivolatile", "partitioning and gas-phase aging of these POA compounds consistent with experimentally derived parameterizations (Murphy et al. 2017), SOA properties and IEPOX organosulfate formation rate constant were updated (Pye et al. 2017). We selected July as representative of summertime and acquired anthropogenic emissions for the CMAQ domain from the MEIC emission inventory 2016 developed by Tsinghua University (http://www.meicmodel.org/), which contains monthly gridded  $(0.25\times 0.25^{\circ})$  emissions information for anthropogenic emissions in the CB05 mechanism. The biogenic emissions were modeled using MEGAN (described in Section 2.2).", "Fig. S1 and S2 show the seasonal spatial distribution of temperature at  $2\mathrm{m}$  (T2) and the daily downward shortwave radiation (DSW) simulated by the WRF model. And these meteorological factors were evaluated using in situ measurement data from 824 and 84 national meteorological sites, respectively. The in situ measurement data are from the China Meteorological Data Sharing Service System (http://data.cma.cn/). Table 2 presents the verification statistics for the average daily T2 and DSW among all sites. As shown in Table 2, the mean error (ME), mean bias (MB), correlation coefficient (r) and root mean square error (RMSE) of station-averaged hourly T2 series are 2.47, -1.26, 0.95 and  $2.59^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. The simulation yields cooling biases of  $-0.98$ ,  $-0.90$ ,  $-1.70$  and  $-1.18^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  in spring, summer, fall and winter, respectively. The ME, MB, r and RMSE values for the annual DSW series are 36.00,  $-29.88$ , 0.80 and  $81.33\mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$ , and the DSW simulation yields underestimations of 32.12, 43.61, 24.25 and  $19.38\mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$  in spring, summer, fall and winter, respectively. It is because that the WRF model overestimate the cloud coverage which lead to the slightly underestimation on T2 and DSW (Wang et al. 2010; Wen et al. 2014). Compared with other studies on yearly long WRF simulation in China (Wang et al., 2014; Ying et al. 2014; Zhang et al., 2012), these biases are", "Fig. 2. Seasonal spatial distribution of the LAI in the model domain.", "between our results and those of Klinger et al. (2002). It is mainly because that Klinger et al. (2002) did not include emissions from the shrubs and assumed that BVOC emissions emitted from forests in China with  $251,283\mathrm{km}^2$  which is smaller than the domain of our study. Therefore, variations in these factors in different years may be the cause of the uncertainty in the modeling simulations. content.title: Figure 4. Effects of a drought on  CO_{2}  emissions from thermal power plants in Yunnan Province are presented in panels (a) and (b). The impact of emission control measures on  SO_{2}  emissions during the 7th CISM Military World Games is shown in (c) and (d). (a) Relative changes in  CO_{2}  emissions in Yunnan Province and other provinces from 2019 to 2020. (b) Relative changes in hydropower generation and thermal power generation in Yunnan Province from 2019 to 2020. Since January and February are usually combined in the statistical data released by the National Bureau of Statistics, they are plotted as a whole here. (c) Daily  SO_{2}  emissions before, during, and after the emission control period in Wuhan, other cities in Hubei, and other provinces except Hubei. The light gray shading covers the periods of short-term emission control. The daily emissions are normalized to the value on October 6. (d) Relative changes in average  SO_{2}  emissions during the emission control period compared to the periods before and after this period.
content.summary: The figure depicts the impact of drought on CO2 emissions and power generation types in Yunnan, and SO2 emissions during the CISM Military World Games in Wuhan and surrounding areas.
content.insight: The figure illustrates notable changes in emission patterns due to environmental factors and control measures, highlighting differences in CO2 and SO2 emissions across regions and timeframes.

图3 工业热源对象空间分布  
Fig. 3 Spatial distribution of industrial heat source objects", "# An Industrial Heat Source Extraction Method: BP Neural Network Using Temperature Feature Template", "Abstract: Aiming at the problem of insufficient quantity and spatial refinement in the extraction of industrial heat source from annual scale thermal anomaly data, a neural network industrial heat source extraction method based on temperature feature template is proposed by using VIIRS active fire data. This study took Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas as the study area, Firstly, according to the spatial aggregation characteristics of industrial heat sources, the heat source objects were divided by the OPTICS algorithm. Secondly, according to the thermal radiation characteristics of the heat sources, the temperature characteristic template of industrial heat sources and non- industrial heat sources were constructed. Finally, the BP neural network was used to extract industrial heat source objects using the temperature feature template and heat source statistical characteristics as parameters. The results show that: (1) the extraction precision of industrial heat source of the neural network algorithm of temperature feature template proposed in this paper reached  $96.31\%$ . Compared with time filtering and logistic regression methods, the extraction precision of industrial heat sources was improved by  $8.45\%$  and  $7.53\%$ , respectively; (2) From 2015 to 2020, the number of industrial heat sources in the six provinces and cities in Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas decreased by  $27.46\%$ . The number of industrial heat source objects and heat anomalies in Hebei Province decreased by  $8.06\%$  and  $7.44\%$  annually, respectively, which was the largest decrease compared with other provinces and cities. The", "concentration of industrial heat sources in Shandong and Tianjin increased by  $25.72\%$  and  $86.64\%$ , respectively, indicating that the industrial transformation and upgrade policies in the two places have achieved remarkable results; (3) Tangshan, Handan, Lvliang, and Changzhi accounted for  $31.37\%$  of the total industrial heat sources in the study area, which are the main cities in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas. The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in seven cities such as Linfen and Taiyuan was higher than those in other cities; The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in 11 cities such as Beijing and Zhoukou was lower than those in other cities; (4) From January to May 2020, the number of industrial heat anomalies in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas remained unchanged or increased compared with the same period in 2019 and 2021. The COVID-19 had no significant impact on the industrial heat source in the study area. The number of industrial heat anomalies in Wuhan in January and February 2020 decreased by more than  $66.67\%$  compared with that in the same period in 2019 and 2021, the number of industrial heat anomalies from March to May 2020 was lower than that in the same period of 2019. The COVID-19 has had a significant impact on industrial heat sources in Wuhan from January to May 2020. This study reflects the current situation and trend of industrial heat source development in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas, which provides a valuable reference for the formulation and adjustment of relevant policies such as reducing energy consumption and improving secondary industry concentration.", "Key words: industrial heat source; OPTICS algorithm; temperature feature template; neural network; VIIRS Active Fire; Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas", "图1基于BP神经网络的工业热源对象提取方法技术流程

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_42", "source_md": "Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\\figure_1.jpg", "title": "Fig. 1. Map of the study domain and definition of urban (red) and rural (gray) regions over the CONUS. The yellow triangle symbols represent the location of ozonesonde stations (BLD: Boulder, HVA: Huntsville). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)", "references": ["To compute both atmospheric concentrations of gaseous and particulate pollutants and first-order sensitivity coefficients, required for conducting inverse modeling, we use the Community Multiscale Air Quality model configured with the decoupled direct method in three dimensions (CMAQ DDM-3D) based on CMAQ model version 5.2 (Cohan et al., 2005; Napelenok et al., 2006), developed and released by the EPA. Our study domain covering the CONUS utilizes  $12\\mathrm{km}$  grid spacing with  $459\\times 299$  grid points (shown in Fig. 1), while the vertical extent between the surface and  $100\\mathrm{hPa}$  is discretized with 27 layers of variable thickness. We use carbon bond (CB6r3) for the gas phase and AERO6 for the aerosol chemical mechanism. We obtained the chemical initial and boundary conditions from the CMAQ model with the in-line dust model covering the entire Northern Hemisphere (HCMAQ) (Mathur et al., 2017), providing dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions in a consistent physical configuration. Details of the HCMAQ simulation and its emission data can be found in Jung et al. (2021) and Vukovich et al. (2018). Since the HCMAQ simulation was based on the year 2016, we applied seasonal scaling for gaseous species based on the comparison", "Using the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model version 4.0, we estimated meteorological fields over the U.S.  $12\\mathrm{km}$  modeling domain (shown in Fig. 1) with the initial and boundary conditions from the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR), which has a  $32\\mathrm{km}$  horizontal resolution and  $3\\mathrm{h}$  of temporal resolution. Detailed configurations of both the CMAQ and WRF models can be found in Table 1. In an effort to enhance model performance in simulating meteorological fields, we applied indirect soil moisture and temperature nudging technique (Pleim and Gilliam, 2009; Pleim and Xiu, 2003) together with a four-", "The variability of satellite-observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns reflects changes in bottom-up emissions and the contribution of background sources in the upper troposphere (Silvern et al., 2019). In Fig. 3, we plotted seasonal averaged changes in observed and simulated tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities in four regions: domainwide (i.e., CONUS), urban and rural regions, and southeastern (SE) areas. We defined an urban area based on the U.S. census-based surrogate, where population density is at least 1000 people per square mile, denoted in red in Fig. 1; and we defined a rural region, designated by gray in Fig. 1, as more than  $20\\mathrm{km}$  away from both urban regions and power plants with a capacity of 1 MW or more, based on the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The southeastern (SE) area (a blue rectangle shown in Fig. 1) denotes an area with dominant lightning-induced (to the total  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions) and biogenic emissions (to the total NMVOCs emissions) over the CONUS. In SE, NMVOCs emissions from biogenic sources are dominant, so we excluded the SE area when we analyzed modeled and observed HCHO columns over urban and rural regions in Fig. 3.", "Fig. 3. The long-term trend in summertime averaged tropospheric (a-1 to h-1)  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and (a-2 to h-2) HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite (2011-2018) and simulated by the CMAQ model (2011, 2014, and 2017), and their relative changes in the CONUS and urban, rural, and southeastern U.S. regions (shown in Fig. 1). CMAQ simulations were conducted with the prior emission and three background setups (with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and lightning emissions (LNO), with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions, and with profile lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions)."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Data & Experimental Setup", "sub_roles": ["Introduce Experimental Setup", "Describe Data Distribution"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["CONUS", "ozonesonde stations", "CMAQ", "WRF", "urban regions", "rural regions", "Boulder", "Huntsville"], "concise_summary": "The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville).", "inferred_insight": "Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.", "paper_id": 7}

content.title: Fig. 1. The distributions of  NO_{x}  emission factors for coal-fired power plants and industrial boilers. The red bars are beyond the  95\%  CIs. The figures represent power generating units: (a) without LNB and bituminous combustion ( <300MW ); (b) without LNB and anthracite combustion ( <300MW ); (c) with LNB and bituminous combustion ( <300MW ); (d) with LNB and anthracite combustion ( <300MW ); (e) with tangential burner and bituminous combustion ( \geq300MW ); (f) with wall-fired burner and bituminous combustion ( \geq300MW ); (g) with tangential burner and anthracite combustion ( \geq300MW ); (h) with wall-fired burner and anthracite combustion ( \geq300MW ); (i) with W-flame burner and anthracite combustion ( \geq300MW ). The last figure (j) represents industrial grate boilers.
content.summary: Distributions of NOx emission factors for various coal-fired power plants and industrial boilers are shown, highlighting emissions beyond 95% confidence intervals.
content.insight: The figure illustrates emission factor distribution discrepancies among different types of coal-fired units, facilitating comparison and analysis based on factors like burner type and boiler capacity.

content.title: Fig. 3. The distributions of estimated  SO_2  emissions from coal-fired power plants in Guizhou in 2005, using Monte Carlo simulation. The black and red bars represent the results from unit-based and sector-based methods, respectively.
content.summary: Comparison of SO2 emission distributions from coal-fired power plants in Guizhou in 2005 using unit-based and sector-based methods via Monte Carlo simulation.
content.insight: Monte Carlo simulation confirms that the sector-based method produces significantly greater uncertainty in SO2 emission estimates than the unit-based method, emphasizing the advantages of detailed source-level data.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_190", "source_md": "Quantifying the uncertainties of a bottom-up emiss.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Quantifying the uncertainties of a bottom-up emiss\\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig. 3. The distributions of estimated  SO_2  emissions from coal-fired power plants in Guizhou in 2005, using Monte Carlo simulation. The black and red bars represent the results from unit-based and sector-based methods, respectively.", "references": ["In this study, such a test is conducted for one province in China as an example, Guizhou, where coal with extremely high sulfur content is used in power generation. As shown in Fig. 2, the  $95\\%$  CIs using the sector-based method is largest for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ $(-30\\% \\sim 108\\%)$  and smallest for  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ $(-25\\% \\sim 20\\%)$ . Compared to  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , the  $95\\%$  CI of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emission is relatively large, reaching  $-40\\% \\sim 42\\%$ , and the variation of sulfur contents is estimated to contribute almost  $90\\%$  to that uncertainty. Figure 3 shows the difference in distributions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions applying the two methods. The test confirms that the uncertainties of provincial and national emissions from power plants may be larger using a sector-based method compared to a unit-based one. Lacking detailed source information, however, such judgment cannot be easily extrapolated to other sectors without similar tests.", "Abstract. The inventory is structured by seven dominant sectors: coal-fired electric power, cement, iron and steel, other industry (boiler combustion), other industry (non-combustion processes), transportation, and residential. For each parameter related to emission factors or activity-level calculations, the uncertainties, represented as probability distributions, are either statistically fitted using results of domestic field tests or, when these are lacking, estimated based on foreign or other domestic data. Due to narrow classification of source types, large sample sizes, and relatively high data quality, the coal-fired power sector is estimated to have the smallest emission uncertainties for all species except BC and OC. content.title: Fig. 5. Comparisons of emission uncertainties for different species in China from different studies, expressed as  95\%  CIs around the central estimates.
content.summary: The figure compares emission uncertainties for various species in China across different studies, showing 95% confidence intervals.
content.insight: This figure illustrates the reduced uncertainties in emission estimates for PM species and carbonaceous compounds due to improved categorizations and data quality.

This large discrepancy may come mainly from the difference between the updated domestic emission factors used in this study, and the foreign emission factor database that Ohara et al. (2007) relied more upon. Figure 5 compares the resulting emission inventory uncertainties with those of other studies, none of which included Monte Carlo simulation. The estimated uncertainty ranges for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions are very similar among the available studies, while those of PM fractions of different sizes or carbonaceous species are significantly reduced. For example, the  $95\\%$  CIs of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions are estimated to be  $-17\\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$  in this study, respectively, much lower than  $-57\\% \\sim 130\\%$ ,  $-68\\% \\sim 208\\%$ , and  $-72\\% \\sim 258\\%$  recently derived by Zhang et al. (2009d). The main reasons include: (1) the source categories in this study are more detailed, and random errors are thus significantly reduced due to the \"compensation-of-error\" mechanism (the same statistical mechanism described for the power sector comparison in Sect. 4.4); and (2) emission factors are", "Abstract. The uncertainties of a national, bottom-up inventory of Chinese emissions of anthropogenic  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , and particulate matter (PM) of different size classes and carbonaceous species are comprehensively quantified, for the first time, using Monte Carlo simulation. The inventory is structured by seven dominant sectors: coal-fired electric power, cement, iron and steel, other industry (boiler combustion), other industry (non-combustion processes), transportation, and residential. For each parameter related to emission factors or activity-level calculations, the uncertainties, represented as probability distributions, are either statistically fitted using results of domestic field tests or, when these are lacking, estimated based on foreign or other domestic data. The uncertainties (i.e.,  $95\\%$  confidence intervals around the central estimates) of Chinese emissions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , total PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , black carbon (BC), and organic carbon (OC) in 2005 are estimated to be  $-14\\% \\sim 13\\%$ ,  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ ,  $-11\\% \\sim 38\\%$ ,  $-14\\% \\sim 45\\%$ ,  $-17\\% \\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$ , respectively. Variations at activity levels (e.g., energy consumption or industrial production) are not the main source of emission uncertainties. Due to narrow classification of source types, large sample sizes, and relatively high data quality, the coal-fired power sector is estimated to have the smallest emission uncertainties for all species except BC and OC. Due to poorer source classifications and a wider range of estimated emission factors, considerable uncertainties of  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  and PM emissions from cement production and boiler combustion in other industries are found. The probability distributions of emission factors for biomass burning, the largest source of BC and OC, are fitted based on very limited domestic field measurements, and special caution should thus be taken interpreting", "Uncertainties introduced by energy statistics and applications of non-Chinese emission factors have been estimated in previous Chinese emission inventory studies. Streets et al. (2003) applied expert judgment, in which the coefficients of variation (i.e., standard deviation divided by the mean) of the activity levels were assumed based on measures of economic development and perceived statistical quality, and those of emission factors were based on the reliability ratings of U.S. emission factors (USEPA, 2002). They estimated that the uncertainties of Chinese emissions varied from  $-12\\% \\sim 13\\%$  for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  to  $-83\\% \\sim 495\\%$  for organic carbon (expressed as the lower and upper bounds of a  $95\\%$  confidence interval, CI, around a central estimate). To investigate discrepancies in the trends of bottom-up  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions and of the  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  column by satellite, Zhang et al. (2007a) set alternative emission scenarios in which energy statistics from different sources and emission factors with different control levels were applied. They concluded that  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions in east-central China in 2004 could range from 7.9 to  $9.3\\mathrm{Tg}$ , and the annual growth rate of emissions from 1995 to 2004 could range from  $5.5\\%$  to  $7.1\\%$ . Other studies conducted preliminary uncertainty analysis of certain species or sectors in China using statistical methods. Bond et al. (2004) assumed that most emission factors of particulate matter (PM) followed lognormal distributions (with the exception of those of diesel vehicles, which had a gamma distribution) and evaluated the uncertainties of global black carbon (BC) and organic carbon (OC) emissions. The  $95\\%$  CIs for Chinese emissions were estimated to be  $-37\\% \\sim 147\\%$  and  $-44\\% \\sim 104\\%$ , respectively. Wu et al. (2010) focused on", "In this study, therefore, the uncertainties (expressed as the  $95\\%$  CI around the central estimate) of a bottom-up emission inventory in China is evaluated with Monte Carlo simulations, combining comprehensive field measurements of domestic emission factors and investigations of activity levels. The species include  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , total PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC. The target year is 2005 because it has the most available data. Determining the probability distributions of all of the related parameters is the main undertaking of this study. Section 2 reviews bottom-up emission inventory methods and analyzes the uncertainties of emission source fractions and corresponding activity levels. Section 3 is a thorough analysis of emission factors by sector and fuel type. Section 4 presents the results and related discussion of emission uncertainties by sector, sensitivity analysis, reliability and limitations of the analysis, and comparisons with other studies. Section 5 summarizes the present study.", "Chinese emissions in 2005 with  $95\\%$  CIs are shown in Table 1 by sector. To be consistent with previous studies, emissions from industrial boilers, cement plants, iron and steel plants, and other industrial processes are aggregated into a single category of industry emissions (see Table S8 in the Supplement for emissions and uncertainties in these sub-sectors). The uncertainties (expressed as the  $95\\%$  CI around the central value) are estimated to be  $-14\\% \\sim 13\\%$ ,  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ ,  $-11\\% \\sim 38\\%$ ,  $-14\\% \\sim 45\\%$ ,  $-17\\% \\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$  for total emissions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC respectively (see Fig. S2 for the output distributions of those species by Monte Carlo simulation).", "the result follow. The PM emission characteristics of residential coal combustion in China were tested by different researchers (Zhang and Smith, 2000; Chen et al., 2005; Zhang et al., 2008b), yielding varied emission factors. Very few estimates of the emission factors of Chinese combustion of biomass can be found, however, except for a series of measurements by Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b). Those measurements were made in four provinces with different types of biomass. As no other domestic tests are available for direct comparisons, systematic biases in these available measurements cannot be ruled out. In this study, we adopt the results from Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b) fully, lacking further domestic evidence. Given potential high uncertainties, however, a comparison test is conducted for OC emitted by its leading source, biomass combustion, in which the uncertainties of emission factors obtained from Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b) are replaced with those of foreign studies (Andreae and Merlet, 2001; Streets et al., 2001; Bond et al., 2004). In this case the  $95\\%$  CI of OC emission from biomass combustion changes from  $-58\\sim 216\\%$  to  $-31\\%\\sim 311\\%$ , suggesting caution in interpretation of results based on limited domestic field measurements (see also the discussion in Sect. 4.3).", "The situations for emission factors with higher reliability (i.e., A and B) are more complicated. For coal-fired power plants, the data on which the probability distributions for most parameters are fitted are obtained from both thorough field measurements by the authors and published studies by others (see details in Zhao et al., 2010). These support different emission factors across burner types, fuel qualities, and emission control levels. The uncertainty analysis for this sector are thus the most reliable in this study. Such confidence, however, does not apply to the residential sector, particularly biomass burning, even though the emission factors meet A or B criteria. As discussed in Sect. 4.1, the data on which the distributions are based are limited in number and systematic bias may result in this sector. In other words, the reliability of uncertainty analysis of biomass burning is in fact poor despite the statistical strength of data fitting. Given its high contributions to uncertainties in emission estimates, more field measurements of emissions from biomass burning in China are urgently needed.", "Fig. 4. Comparisons of annual emissions for China from different studies. The emissions are for year 2005 unless noted. The range line represents the  $95\\%$  CIs around the central estimates of this study.", "Although inverse studies that evaluate emission inventories using observations and CTMs have indicated that Chinese  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions may be underestimated by around  $50\\%$  (Wang et al., 2004; Ma et al., 2006), the uncertainty estimated in this study is relatively small, ranging only  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ . The difference may result in part from omission in bottom-up inventories of anthropogenic  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  sources of entirely different character, such as microbial decomposition of organic wastes associated with the human-animal food chain and applications of chemical fertilizer (Wang et al., 2004). Moreover, emissions vary considerably across regions of China (Wang et al., 2007; Zhao and Wang, 2009) and seasons (Zhang et al., 2007a), and emission uncertainties differentiated accordingly may be larger than national estimates. In order to compare better emission estimates derived from satellite, aircraft, or surface observations, therefore, further investigation of the uncertainties of bottom-up inventories by regions and seasons is needed. These would require region-specific uncertainty assumptions for emission factors and activity levels, and seasonal distribution parameters."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Compare Experimental Performance", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["emission uncertainties", "SO2", "NOx", "PM10", "PM2.5", "BC", "OC", "China", "Monte Carlo simulation"], "concise_summary": "The figure compares emission uncertainties for various species in China across different studies, showing 95% confidence intervals.", "inferred_insight": "This figure illustrates the reduced uncertainties in emission estimates for PM species and carbonaceous compounds due to improved categorizations and data quality.", "paper_id": 29}

content.title: Fig. 1. The frequency of sulfur and ash contents in coal and that of removal efficiencies of  SO_2  and  NO_X  for Jiangsu's power plants and other industrial boilers, based on plant-by-plant investigation. The plant numbers are indicated in the parentheses.
content.summary: Fig. 1 illustrates the frequency distribution of sulfur and ash contents in coal, and removal efficiencies of SO2 and NOX for Jiangsu's power plants and industrial boilers.
content.insight: The figure highlights variability in sulfur and ash content across plants and compares the effectiveness of SO2 and NOX removal systems, underscoring the importance of plant-specific data in emissions analysis.
