metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_262", "source_md": "Quantifying the uncertainties of China's emission.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Quantifying the uncertainties of China's emission\\figure_1.jpg", "title": "Fig. 1. The frequency of sulfur and ash contents in coal and that of removal efficiencies of  SO_2  and  NO_X  for Jiangsu's power plants and other industrial boilers, based on plant-by-plant investigation. The plant numbers are indicated in the parentheses.", "supplement": "(a) For power plants;(b) For other industrial boilers;(c) For power plants;(d) For other industrial boilers", "references": ["To quantify the uncertainties of regional (provincial and city) inventories, Monte Carlo framework was improved by incorporating plant-specific information and then applied at each plant for point sources. As the energy consumption data of power sector were compiled and crosschecked based on multiple data sources (Zhou et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2015), we assumed that the uncertainty would be smaller than other sources and we tentatively set the CV at  $10\\%$  for each individual plant. For point sources of other categories and area sources, in contrast, the CVs of activity data were set at  $20\\%$ , the same as the national inventory. For emission factors, we divided the parameters into two types, i.e., the common ones and plant-specific ones. The common parameters indicated those that keep stable or change slightly for certain technology/fuel type, e.g., the sulfur and ash release ratio of bituminous pulverized combustion. The PDFs were determined following Zhao et al. (2011, 2013), with the most recent measurement data incorporated. The plant-specific parameters indicated those that are independent and might vary largely between different plants, e.g., the  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  removal efficiencies of flue gas desulfurization (FGD) and selective catalytic reduction (SCR) systems, respectively, and the sulfur and ash contents of fossil fuels. Available information was collected and comprehensively compiled to generate the PDFs for those parameters. Details are discussed in the following section.", "Plant-specific parameters were applied mainly in uncertainty analysis of point sources in regional inventories (for area sources and point sources without detailed information, common parameters were applied instead). For sources related to fossil fuel combustion (e.g., power plants, industrial boilers/kilns, and industrial processes that contain coal combustion), the PDFs of sulfur content, ash content, and  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO_X}$  removal efficiencies of corresponding air pollutant control devices (APCDs) were determined for each plant. Fig. 1a and b shows the frequencies of sulfur and ash contents for Jiangsu's power plants and other industrial boilers. The average sulfur content was lower for coals used for power generation than those for other boilers. Normal distributions were assumed for sulfur and ash contents of coal burned for individual plant. Standard deviations of those distributions were determined for each plant with following steps. First, standard deviation for the whole sector was determined through normal distribution fitting with all the data samples within the sector. The difference between sulfur/ash content of single plant and average of the whole sector was then calculated for each plant. The larger value obtained from above two steps was selected as the standard deviation for single plant.", "Fig. 1c and d shows the frequencies of removal efficiencies of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO_X}$  for Jiangsu's power and other industrial plants. For both source categories,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  control systems were more widely applied than  $\\mathrm{NO_X}$  control, while the average  $\\mathrm{NO_X}$  removal efficiency based on limited data samples was higher than that of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  for other industrial boilers. Triangular distribution was assumed for the  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  removal efficiency for each plant. Clear difference was found between sectors. In 2012,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  control technologies (including wetFGD and other simple technologies) were applied at  $90\\%$  of Jiangsu's coal-fired power units, with the average removal efficiency calculated at  $68\\%$ . For industrial boilers and kilns, in contrast,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  control was applied only  $18\\%$  of the plants and the average removal rate was  $42\\%$ . Thus the ranges of triangular distributions differed between source categories. For power sector, the ranges of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  removal rates for FGD systems and other technologies (e.g., circulating fluidized bed, CFB) were  $50 - 98\\%$  and  $10 - 85\\%$ , respectively, according to the investigated data. For other industrial boilers and kilns, similarly, the ranges were determined at  $50 - 98\\%$ ,  $30 - 85\\%$ ,  $10 - 60\\%$ , and  $10 - 85\\%$  for wet-FGD, dry-FGD, CFB and other technologies, respectively. Besides coal combustion, FGD systems were also applied for other industrial processes. Relatively high removal efficiencies were found for sintering in iron & steel production, and a triangular distribution ranged  $50 - 98\\%$  was assumed for individual plant. For other processes including production of ammonia,", "Table 4 summarized the 1st and 2nd ranking parameters contributing most to the uncertainties of Jiangsu's and Nanjing's emissions in the provincial and city-level inventory by industrial source category, respectively. There were differences in the key parameter identification for the province in the national and provincial inventory. For  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  from power sector, as an example, the removal efficiencies of FGD for given individual plants were identified as the most significant parameters to the uncertainty, with very limited contribution to variance. Similarly, the uncertainty of emissions from other industrial combustions was estimated to be mostly affected by the sulfur content and FGD removal rate of one single plant. The results thus indicated the importance of better understanding on emission characteristics for certain individual"], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["Jiangsu", "power plants", "industrial boilers", "sulfur content", "ash content", "SO2 removal efficiency", "NOX removal efficiency"], "concise_summary": "Fig. 1 illustrates the frequency distribution of sulfur and ash contents in coal, and removal efficiencies of SO2 and NOX for Jiangsu's power plants and industrial boilers.", "inferred_insight": "The figure highlights variability in sulfur and ash content across plants and compares the effectiveness of SO2 and NOX removal systems, underscoring the importance of plant-specific data in emissions analysis.", "paper_id": 28}

content.title: Fig. 10. Relative difference between the SOA concentrations averaged in July with and without biogenic emissions ug/m3
content.summary: Map showing relative difference in SOA concentrations in July with and without biogenic emissions over China.
content.insight: Biogenic emissions are significant contributors to SOA during summer in China, impacting air quality, particularly in urban areas where controlling anthropogenic emissions could help reduce SOA levels.

Relative difference between the SOA concentrations averaged in July with and without biogenic emissions ug/m3", "references": ["As shown in Fig. 10, biogenic emissions are the most important contributors to SOA in summer. In China, biogenic emissions account for approximately  $70\\%$  of SOA. In most areas, biogenic emissions are the most crucial contributors; even in areas where there are no significant isoprene emissions, the relative contribution of biogenic", "Biogenic volatile organic compounds (BVOC) play an important role in global environmental chemistry and climate. In the present work, biogenic emissions from China in 2017 were estimated based on the Model of Emissions of Gases and Aerosols from Nature (MEGAN). The effects of BVOC emissions on ozone and secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation were investigated using the WRF-CMAQ modeling system. Three parallel scenarios were developed to assess the impact of BVOC emissions on China's ozone and SOA formation in July 2017. Biogenic emissions were estimated at  $23.54\\mathrm{Tg / yr}$ , with a peak in the summer and decreasing from southern to northern China. The high BVOC emissions across eastern and southwestern China increased the surface ozone levels, particularly in the BTH (Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei), SCB (Sichuan Basin), YRD (Yangtze River Delta) and central PRD (Pearl River Delta) regions, with increases of up to  $47\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  due to the sensitivity of VOC-limited urban areas. In summer, most SOA concentrations formed over China are from biogenic sources (national average of  $70\\%$ ). And SOA concentrations in YRD and SCB regions are generally higher than other regions. Excluding anthropogenic emissions while keeping biogenic emissions unchanged results that SOA concentrations reduce by  $60\\%$  over China, which indicates that anthropogenic emissions can interact with biogenic emissions then facilitate biogenic SOA formation. It is suggested that controlling anthropogenic emissions would result in reduction of both anthropogenic and biogenic SOA.", "As shown in Fig. 7d-e, ozone concentration simulated by NB case is significantly higher than NA case which indicated that effect of biogenic emissions on ozone concentration is less than that of anthropogenic emissions. To clarify the change in ozone concentration after removal of BVOC, Fig. 8 shows the relative difference in ozone concentration with/without BVOC emissions. The high BVOC emissions across the eastern and southwestern areas of China increased  $\\mathrm{DM1O_3}$", "Fig. 9 presents the SOA concentrations simulated by base case, NA and NB case, respectively. In summer, SOA concentrations in central and eastern China typically exceed  $1\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and can reach up to  $3\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . There is also a relatively high SOA concentration in other southern provinces and the Sichuan Basin. The simulated SOA concentrations are low in northern and northeastern China. Removing anthropogenic VOC emissions in July causes a decrease of SOA in major areas by relative change of approximately  $60\\%$  while eliminating biogenic emissions results in an approximately  $70\\%$  reduction of the simulated SOA concentrations. Therefore, the removal of biogenic emissions affects both biogenic and anthropogenic compounds of SOA.", "Fig. 8. Relative difference between the surface  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  averaged in July with and without biogenic emissions (monthly average of the daily 1-h maximum,  $\\mu \\mathrm{g} \\, \\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ )."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["SOA concentrations", "biogenic emissions", "China", "BVOC", "ozone", "MEGAN", "WRF-CMAQ", "Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei", "Sichuan Basin", "Yangtze River Delta", "Pearl River Delta"], "concise_summary": "Map showing relative difference in SOA concentrations in July with and without biogenic emissions over China.", "inferred_insight": "Biogenic emissions are significant contributors to SOA during summer in China, impacting air quality, particularly in urban areas where controlling anthropogenic emissions could help reduce SOA levels.", "paper_id": 13}

content.title: Fig. 3. The emission factor of the total PM, and for its chemical constituents as a function of fuel sulphur content (mass-based percentage), based on the data from the second IMO GHG study (Buhaug et al., 2009). Lines indicate the PM component emission factors in STEAM2. The emission factors of the total PM,  SO_4  and  H_2O  are linearly dependent on the fuel sulphur content. The scale used for total PM,  SO_4  and associated  H_2O  is different (left-hand axis) from the scale used for EC, OC and ash (right-hand axis).
content.summary: The figure illustrates the relationship between fuel sulphur content and emission factors of total PM and its constituents, using data from the STEAM2 model and Buhaug study.
content.insight: The linear dependence of total PM, SO4, and associated H2O emission factors on sulphur content implies reductions in sulphur content could significantly decrease these emissions, despite constant emissions from EC, OC, and ash.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_104", "source_md": "Extension of an assessment model of ship traffic e.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Extension of an assessment model of ship traffic e\\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig. 3. The emission factor of the total PM, and for its chemical constituents as a function of fuel sulphur content (mass-based percentage), based on the data from the second IMO GHG study (Buhaug et al., 2009). Lines indicate the PM component emission factors in STEAM2. The emission factors of the total PM,  SO_4  and  H_2O  are linearly dependent on the fuel sulphur content. The scale used for total PM,  SO_4  and associated  H_2O  is different (left-hand axis) from the scale used for EC, OC and ash (right-hand axis).", "references": ["The sulphur content of the fuel has a crucial influence on the PM emissions. The dependency of PM emission factor on fuel sulphur content was modelled according to Buhaug et al. (2009), as presented in Fig. 3. The emission factors of the total PM,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4$  and associated  $\\mathrm{H}_2\\mathrm{O}$  (i.e.  $\\mathrm{H}_2\\mathrm{O}$  attached to sulphate) are assumed to be linearly dependent on the fuel sulphur content, whereas the emission factors of EC, OC and ash are independent of this factor in STEAM2. The emissions of PM could therefore not be eradicated totally, even if sulphur would be completely eliminated from ship fuels (Winnes and Fridell, 2010b; Buhaug et al., 2009). The measured total mass of particulate matter as defined here includes also the associated  $\\mathrm{H}_2\\mathrm{O}$ ; the amount of which may substantially vary according to the experimental set-up and conditions during the exhaust measurements."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart", "Scatter Plot"]}, "key_entities": ["Emission Factor", "Total PM", "SO4", "H2O", "Fuel Sulphur Content", "STEAM2", "Buhaug"], "concise_summary": "The figure illustrates the relationship between fuel sulphur content and emission factors of total PM and its constituents, using data from the STEAM2 model and Buhaug study.", "inferred_insight": "The linear dependence of total PM, SO4, and associated H2O emission factors on sulphur content implies reductions in sulphur content could significantly decrease these emissions, despite constant emissions from EC, OC, and ash.", "paper_id": 17}

content.title: Fig. 7. Percentages  \%  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), reactivity  (L_{OH)} , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP) under different  PM_{2.5}  concentration ranges.
content.summary: The figure shows percentage contributions of various VOC sources to VOC mixing ratio, reactivity, ozone formation potential, and secondary organic aerosol potential over different PM2.5 concentration ranges.
content.insight: The analysis demonstrates the rising significance of industrial sources of VOCs in conditions of high PM2.5 pollution, highlighting their impact on chemical reactivity and pollution potential.

{"id": "img_204", "source_md": "Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds\figure_7.jpg", "title": "Fig. 7. Percentages  \%  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), reactivity  (L_{OH)} , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP) under different  PM_{2.5}  concentration ranges.", "references": ["To better understand the differences in levels and reactivity of VOC sources under different air quality, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels were classified into several groups and the source contributions, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP of VOCs were summarized in Fig. 7. Generally, with the increase of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels from clean to heavy pollution, the VOC levels also increased from  $40.8 \pm 17.6$  ppbv to  $89.6 \pm 26.3$  ppbv. The same phenomenon was also reported elsewhere (Han et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2017; Hui et al., 2019). Fig. 7 clearly shows that the high  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution was characterized by an increase of industrial sources, which accounted for up to  $27.3\%$ ,  $56.2\%$ ,  $48.4\%$ , and  $57.0\%$  of VOCs mixing ratio, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP, respectively. The results here were similar to a previous study that the percentage of industrial sources increased significantly with the increase in VOC levels and this source dominated the VOC origins during an entire severe pollution episode (Hui et al.,", "Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are important contributors to atmospheric chemical reactivity rate  $(\mathrm{L_{OH}})$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol formation potential (SOAP). The purpose of this study was to determine the contributions of different VOC sources to  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP using positive matrix factorization (PMF) model. Hourly observation of ambient VOCs at a megacity of Central China was conducted for one month in winter. The  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP of VOC species were firstly estimated. The PMF model was then used to apportion their sources. Five VOC sources including solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, combustion sources, vehicle emissions, and industrial sources were apportioned with their average contributions of  $11.6\%$ ,  $14.2\%$ ,  $39.0\%$ ,  $22.5\%$ , and  $12.6\%$ , respectively. Industrial sources were the highest contributors to the  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$  and SOAP with average contributions of  $32.2\%$  and  $32.8\%$ , respectively, while vehicle emissions contributed most to the OFP  $(27.8\%)$ . This study highlighted that the VOCs and ozone pollution control should give priority to the reactivity (OFP and SOAP) based strategies rather than concentration contribution-based strategies.", "Fig. 6. Percentages  $(\%)$  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), OH loss rate  $\mathrm{(L_{OH})}$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP).", "The limitations of this study were from several aspects: one is the limited known chemistry properties of VOC species; the other is the underestimation of the current modelling; and the third is the assumption of the PMF model. For instance, the ozone reactivity scales for 118 VOC species under different  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  conditions were summarized by Carter (2013). Similarly, Atkinson and Arey (2003) summarized the atmospheric degradation of about 190 VOC species (known as the rate constant  $k_{OH}$ ). A list of the SOAPs relative to toluene for 113 VOC species was also reported by Derwent et al. (2010). Despite the great efforts have been made to study the photochemical characteristics of the VOC species, some VOC species' properties are still unknown. They may be important to the atmospheric reactivity, ozone, and secondary aerosol formation potentials. Limited species (i.e., 33 species in this study) introduced into the PMF model suggested the underestimation of  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP. For SOA modelling, the SOA yield method using chamber study or simulation using volatility basis sets (VBS) underestimated the SOA mass (Chen et al., 2017). For the estimation of OFP, despite the MIR method considers the chemical mechanisms and impacts of the  $\mathrm{VOC} / \mathrm{NO}_x$  ratio on ozone production, it may also introduce simulation uncertainties (Ran et al., 2009).", "the flaws and uncertainty existed, this study also provided an additional approach to understand the contributions of various VOC sources to the reactivity of OH loss rate, ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP) using the PMF model.", "Dumanoglu, Y., Kara, M., Altiok, H., Odabasi, M., Elbir, T., Bayram, A., 2014. content.title: Figure 7. Emission ratios of  SO_2  to  CO_{2} , and CO to  CO_{2}  by Asian regions. CHN is China, OEA is Other East Asia, IND is India, OSA is Other South Asia, SEA is Southeast Asia, CA is Central Asia, and RA is Russia Asia.
content.summary: The figure shows emission ratios of SO2 to CO2 and CO to CO2 across Asian regions, highlighting variations in emission characteristics.
content.insight: The ratios suggest combustion efficiency and emission control levels, with higher SO2/CO2 indicating coal use and low controls, while CO/CO2 shows combustion efficiency discrepancies, particularly low in India's and Southeast Asia's residential sectors.

", "total primary energy consumption of Asia has increased by $20.6\\%$ during the period of 2005 and 2010 (IEA, 2013). During the same period, $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions decreased in China $(-17.2\\%)$ , Other East Asia $(-9.5\\%)$ , and Central Asia $(-32.8\\%)$ due to effective emission control, while they increased in India $(+23.9\\%)$ , Other South Asia $(+23.9\\%)$ , and Southeast Asia $(+7.8\\%)$ due to growth in coal use and absence of desulfurization devices. The decrease in $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions changes in Asian is dominated by changes in China and India. Figure 8a demonstrates the changes in $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions among Asian regions from 2006 to 2010. Wide installation of FGD in China's coal-fired power plants is the main driving factor of $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ emission changes over Asia. $\\mathrm{SO}_2$", "dustrial activities and vehicle population. For $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , remarkable emission increases are observed for China (+22.6%), India (+27.8%), Other South Asia (+20.5%), and Southeast Asia (+23.4%) during 2006-2010. For NMVOC, emissions increased by 14.0, 15.0, 12.3, 24.9, 23.6, and 9.3% for China, India, Other South Asia, Southeast Asia, Central Asia, and Russia Asia respectively. Emission changes of other species are relatively small (i.e., within 6%) during 2006-2010. For CO, $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ , and $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , emission reductions in China were partly offset by increases of emissions in the South and Southeast Asian regions. CO emissions in China decreased by 5% during 2006-2010 (see Fig. 8b), mainly due to improved combustion efficiency, recycling of industrial coal gases, and strengthened vehicle emission standards. The implementation of new vehicle emission standards and retirement of old vehicles has reduced China's transportation CO and NMVOC emissions by 20 and 30%, respectively, during 2006-2010. While in India, Other South Asia, and Southeast Asia, CO emissions increased by 21, 11, and 16%, respectively, between 2006 and 2010.", "+13.6% compared to MIX) but lower emissions for industry $(-293\\mathrm{Tg}, -6.8\\%)$ emissions. EDGAR v4.2 estimates lower $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ emissions for China $(-308\\mathrm{Tg}, -3.4\\%)$ and higher emissions for other regions: +102Tg (+5.6%) for Other East Asia, +83.9Tg (+5.6%) for Southeast Asia, +204Tg (+9.7%) for India, +29.5Tg (+7.5%) for Other South Asia, and +25.3Tg (+6.4%) for Central Asia. At sector level, EDGAR v4.2 estimates are 29% (+833 Tg) higher for power, 22% (-944Tg) lower for industry, and 31% (-192 Tg) lower for transportation over China compared to MIX. For Other East Asia, differences between EDGAR v4.2 and MIX are mainly contributed by power sector, with 20% higher emissions (+130 Tg) in EDGAR v4.2. Residential sector is the main contributor to the differences between EDGAR v4.2 and MIX over India, with 28% (+177 Tg) higher emissions estimated in EDGAR v4.2. The relatively large discrepancy at sector level can be attributed to differences in energy statistics and emission factors (Guan et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2015) as well as differences in sector definitions. In particularly, EDGAR v4.2 used fuel consumption data from IEA statistics while MIX and REAS2 used provincial level data from Chinese Energy Statistics, which can differ by 20% at sector level (Hong et al., 2016). Emissions from heating plants are aggregated to the industrial sector in the MIX inventory, while in EDGAR v4.2 heating plants"], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Compare Experimental Performance", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["SO2", "CO2", "CO", "China", "India", "Other East Asia", "Other South Asia", "Southeast Asia", "Central Asia", "Russia Asia", "MIX inventory"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows emission ratios of SO2 to CO2 and CO to CO2 across Asian regions, highlighting variations in emission characteristics.", "inferred_insight": "The ratios suggest combustion efficiency and emission control levels, with higher SO2/CO2 indicating coal use and low controls, while CO/CO2 shows combustion efficiency discrepancies, particularly low in India's and Southeast Asia's residential sectors.", "paper_id": 23}

content.title: Fig. 5. Monthly BVOC (four types) emissions in China in 2017.
content.summary: Monthly maps showing BVOC emissions in China for 2017, detailing four compound types across seasons.
content.insight: The figure highlights how BVOC emissions in China peak during summer, significantly impacting air quality and climatic conditions.

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_85", "source_md": "Estimation of biogenic VOC emissions and their cor.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Estimation of biogenic VOC emissions and their cor\figure_5.jpg", "title": "Fig. 5. Monthly BVOC (four types) emissions in China in 2017.", "references": ["The modeling results from this study indicate different seasonal BVOC emissions in China (Fig. 5). Isoprene and terpene are the most dominant BVOC emissions. The quantity of seasonal BVOC emissions varies as follows: summer  $>$  spring  $>$  autumn  $>$  winter. The distribution pattern of isoprene in spring and autumn is quite consistent", "particularly in BTH, YRD, SCB and central PRD, by up to  $47\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . This phenomenon is likely due to the combined effects of BVOC emissions and  $\mathrm{O_3 - NOx - VOC}$  sensitivity in these regions. As shown in Fig. 5,", "Biogenic volatile organic compounds (BVOC) play an important role in global environmental chemistry and climate. In the present work, biogenic emissions from China in 2017 were estimated based on the Model of Emissions of Gases and Aerosols from Nature (MEGAN). The effects of BVOC emissions on ozone and secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation were investigated using the WRF-CMAQ modeling system. Three parallel scenarios were developed to assess the impact of BVOC emissions on China's ozone and SOA formation in July 2017. Biogenic emissions were estimated at  $23.54\mathrm{Tg / yr}$ , with a peak in the summer and decreasing from southern to northern China. The high BVOC emissions across eastern and southwestern China increased the surface ozone levels, particularly in the BTH (Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei), SCB (Sichuan Basin), YRD (Yangtze River Delta) and central PRD (Pearl River Delta) regions, with increases of up to  $47\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  due to the sensitivity of VOC-limited urban areas. In summer, most SOA concentrations formed over China are from biogenic sources (national average of  $70\%$ ). And SOA concentrations in YRD and SCB regions are generally higher than other regions. Excluding anthropogenic emissions while keeping biogenic emissions unchanged results that SOA concentrations reduce by  $60\%$  over China, which indicates that anthropogenic emissions can interact with biogenic emissions then facilitate biogenic SOA formation. It is suggested that controlling anthropogenic emissions would result in reduction of both anthropogenic and biogenic SOA.", "In this study, the objectives are to estimate BVOC emissions in China at a high spatial resolution and probe the effects of BVOC emissions on ground-level ozone and SOA formation in China. MEGAN version 2.1 and Weather Research and Forecasting model coupled with the Community Multiscale Air Quality (WRF-CMAQ model) were adopted to estimate BVOC emissions and investigate the impacts of BVOC emissions on ground-level ozone and SOA. The year 2017 was selected as the base year to simulate the spatiotemporal variations in BVOC emissions. The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces the methodologies and databases used to estimate biogenic emissions, as well as the WRF-CMAQ framework. The model performance is described in Section 3, with comparisons of meteorological data on BVOC emissions with model results. Moreover, the effects of BVOC emissions on summertime  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA formation are described in Section 3. The conclusions are presented in Section 4.", "MEGAN v2.1 (Guenther et al. 2012) was utilized to estimate BVOC emissions in China with a  $27\mathrm{-km}$  horizontal grid spacing domain (shown in Fig. 1). The inputs for MEGAN include the LAI, PFTs, emission factors, and meteorological data (e.g., solar radiation, temperature, relative humidity and soil moisture). The monthly average LAI was obtained from the 8-day MODIS LAI product (MOD15A2, 2017) with the same horizontal resolution as the study domain, as shown in Fig. 2. The PFT map was obtained from the MODIS MCD12Q1 product and regridded into the WRF domain to compute the fraction of each PFT in each grid (Fig. 3). The default MEGAN emission factors  $(\mathrm{mg~m}^{-2}\mathrm{h}^{-1})$  with a resolution of  $30\mathrm{s}$ $(< 1\mathrm{km})$ , which reflect the BVOC emission rate under standard canopy conditions, were leveraged (http://lar.wsu.edu/megan/guides.html). Meteorological conditions were simulated by the WRF model. A one-year MEGAN simulation was performed for 2017 in China.", "Table 3 illustrates the estimated annual emissions of BVOCs in this work and other studies. The estimate of annual BVOC emissions was  $23.54\mathrm{Tg}$ , which is within the range reported in previous estimates, ranging from  $12.83\mathrm{Tg}$  to  $42.5\mathrm{Tg}$  between 1990 and 2006. Estimates in the present work are greater than the  $20.6\mathrm{Tg}$  for 2000 estimated by Klinger et al. (2002) and  $12.83\mathrm{Tg}$  for 2003 estimated by Chi and Xie (2011). However, the estimates in the present study are lower than the results of Guenther et al. (1995) and Li et al. (2013), who obtained values of  $28.4\mathrm{Tg}$  for 1990 and  $42.5\mathrm{Tg}$  for 2003, respectively. Differences among the discussed studies can be attributed to various factors. It is reported that the forest coverage, the percentage of which increased from  $16.6\%$  in 2003 to  $21.63\%$  in 2014, and the forest area increased from  $1.59\times 10^{8}\mathrm{hm}^{2}$  in 2003 to  $2.08\times 10^{8}\mathrm{hm}^{2}$  in 2014 (China Forestry Bureau (CFB), 2014), which may be the main reasons for large discrepancies. In addition, BVOC emissions were largely followed by LAI changes (Souri et al. 2017). Chen et al. (2019) utilized satellite data to analyze the annual average MODIS LAI in China from 2000 to 2017 and observed a significant increasing trend in eastern China (the trend was higher than  $18\times 10^{-2}\mathrm{m}^2$  per  $\mathrm{m}^2$  per decade; see Fig. S3 in the Supporting Information). Therefore, the interannual variation of LAI is also an important factor which cause the differences. Besides, some of the discrepancies can also be explained by the use of different algorithms and emission factors. In this study, the MEGAN default global average EFs were applied to estimate BVOC emissions. Li et al. (2012) used a simplified isoprene algorithm (PCEEA) and neglected the effect of soil moisture and detailed canopy information, which resulted in a much lower estimate of  $12.97\mathrm{Tg}$  in 2006 than our study. Yu and Hong (2012) adopted lower EFs and leaf biomass densities for each plant type derived from a small number of local measurements, which caused an underestimation of emissions. There is a large difference of isoprene emission", "and mainly concentrated in southern China due to the relatively dense vegetation cover in southern China. Additionally, decreasing temperature and solar radiation from south to north contribute to the distribution of isoprene emissions in southern China. In summer, eastern China has high temperatures, intense radiation and vigorous forest growth, subsequently resulting in obviously higher BVOC emissions. Except for Inner Mongolia, which is dominated by grassland desert, the monthly emission intensities of isoprene and terpene are  $30 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~g}/$  month or higher in most areas. Due to dieback of vegetation and the", "Fig. 6 illustrates the different monthly evolution patterns of BVOC emissions in China. BVOC emissions are concentrated between April and September and reach a maximum of  $5.8\mathrm{Tg}$  in June. content.title: Figure 4. Annual BVOC emissions in China from 2001 to 2016 for five scenarios (S1-S5) described in Table 1. The increasing trends and the probabilities  p  using the Mann-Kendall test are shown in the legend.
content.summary: Figure 4 illustrates annual BVOC emissions in China from 2001 to 2016 across five scenarios, highlighting trends and significance using the Mann-Kendall test.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates significant increasing trends in BVOC emissions, notably in scenario S2, without considering meteorological variability, emphasizing the influence of the LAI increase over the period.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_3", "source_md": "A long-term estimation of biogenic volatile organi.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\A long-term estimation of biogenic volatile organi\\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Figure 4. Annual BVOC emissions in China from 2001 to 2016 for five scenarios (S1-S5) described in Table 1. The increasing trends and the probabilities  p  using the Mann-Kendall test are shown in the legend.", "references": ["As shown in Table 2, the average annual emissions from 2001-2016 of isoprene, monoterpene, sesquiterpene and other VOCs estimated from S1 are 15.94 (±1.12), 3.99 (±0.17), 0.50 (±0.03) and 13.84 (±0.78) Tg, respectively. Isoprene is the dominant species and accounts for about half of the total BVOC emission in China. As shown in Fig. 4, the estimated BVOC emission in S2 has a statistically significant increasing trend without considering the annual variability of meteorological conditions. The increasing rates of isoprene, monoterpenes, sesquiterpenes and total BVOC emission in S2 scenarios are 0.64, 0.44, 0.39 and  $0.50\\% \\mathrm{yr}^{-1}$ , respectively. The S1 scenario considers the impact of annual meteorological variability as well as the surface vegetation change, and the BVOC emission in S1 shows an upward trend but did not pass the significance test of  $p < 0.1$ . There is no significant trend of BVOC emission for S3 or S4, with fixed land cover and annually updated meteorological conditions, which demonstrates that meteorology was not the direct driver of the BVOC emission trend in China during this period. Climatic conditions could affect the BVOC emission indirectly by affecting the growth of vegetation and controlling BVOC emission (Peñuelas et al., 2009), which is not considered in the model used in this study. The estimated total BVOC emission in S5 also has a statistically significant increasing trend of  $0.26\\% \\mathrm{yr}^{-1}$  ( $p < 0.05$ ) without considering the annual variability of meteorological conditions, which is purely caused by the increase in LAI from 2001-2016. The changing trend of BVOC in the full scenario cannot be treated by the linear summation of trends in other one-factor scenarios. On the one hand, the response of isoprene emission to meteorological conditions is nonlinear (Guenther et al., 1993). On the other hand, the calculation of national-scale total emission amount is affected by the spatial variabilities of vegetation types as well as climatic conditions, and it should not be a linear combination of the two aspects.", "the estimation of monoterpene emission is about  $23.5\\%$  and  $31.3\\%$  higher than that from CAMS-GLOB-BIO v3.1 and v1.1, respectively. We further compared our results with two versions of CAMS-GLOB-BIO inventories. Figures 10 and 11 present the trends of isoprene emission and monoterpene emission, respectively, from S1 and S3 in this study and the CAMS-GLOB-BIO inventory v1.1 and v3.1 from 2001-2016. As shown in Figs. 10 and 11, S3 shows similar spatial patterns and magnitude of the changing trend of isoprene and monoterpene emissions as CAMS-GLOB-BIO v1.1 and CAMS-GLOB-BIO v3.1, e.g., three datasets all showed a strong increasing trend in Yunnan Province, and S1 shows much stronger changing trends comparing with the other three datasets with annually updated LAI and PFT datasets. The meteorological inputs for CAMS-GLOB-BIO v1.1 and v3.1 are ERA-Interim and ERA-5 reanalysis data, respectively, and the WRF model used in this study was also driven by ERA-Interim reanalysis data. Therefore, the four datasets have the same source of meteorological inputs. In addition, these estimations all adopted the same PFT-level emission factors from Guenther et al. (2012). Therefore, the potential reason for the differences of isoprene and monoterpene emission among the datasets in Figs. 10 and 11 is the discrepancies of PFT and LAI inputs. CAMS-GLOB-BIO also adopted the annually updated LAI inputs developed by Yuan et al. (2011) based on the MODIS MOD15A v5 LAI product, but the two versions of the CAMS-GLOB-BIO inventory did not show the same level of strong increasing trend as S1. The increasing trend of LAI in China is confirmed by multiple LAI products but with different rates (Piao et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2020). In this study, we adopted the latest MODIS LAI product of version 6, and a strong increasing trend of LAI in China has been found by using this product (Chen et al., 2019). Therefore, an increasing trend of BVOC emission induced by LAI should be seen in the estimation with annually updated LAI inputs, but the magnitude of this trend is also affected by the magnitude of changing trend of LAI products. The PFT map used in this study is coming from the MODIS land cover product, which is a mesoscale satellite product with the highest resolution of  $500\\mathrm{m}$ . Besides the quality of the product, the method for converting the original land cover classification system to a PFT classification system is also important. Hartley et al. (2017) illustrated that the cross-walking table for converting land cover class maps to PFT fractional maps can lead to  $20\\% -90\\%$  uncertainties for gross primary production estimation in land surface models by using different vegetation fractions for mixed pixels, and the BVOC emission estimation has the same issue. In this study, we assumed that the pixels that were assigned as vegetation are  $100\\%$  covered by that kind of vegetation (Table S1 in the Supplement). Therefore, this will lead to an overestimation of vegetation cover rate for mixed pixels, which can lead to higher BVOC emission."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["BVOC emissions", "isoprene", "monoterpenes", "sesquiterpenes", "scenarios", "Mann-Kendall test", "China"], "concise_summary": "Figure 4 illustrates annual BVOC emissions in China from 2001 to 2016 across five scenarios, highlighting trends and significance using the Mann-Kendall test.", "inferred_insight": "The figure demonstrates significant increasing trends in BVOC emissions, notably in scenario S2, without considering meteorological variability, emphasizing the influence of the LAI increase over the period.", "paper_id": 1}

content.title: Fig. 4. Annual emissions of isoprene, terpene, other VOCs and total BVOCs in 2017.
content.summary: The figure presents maps of annual emissions of isoprene, terpene, other VOCs, and total BVOCs in China for 2017, indicating higher emissions in northeastern, southeastern, and southern regions.
content.insight: The maps highlight the geographical distribution of BVOC emissions in China, emphasizing high emissions in regions with significant forest cover. This correlates with BVOC's role in climate and environmental chemistry.

{"id": "img_84", "source_md": "Estimation of biogenic VOC emissions and their cor.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Estimation of biogenic VOC emissions and their cor\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig. 4. Annual emissions of isoprene, terpene, other VOCs and total BVOCs in 2017.", "supplement": "(a).isoprene;(b).terpene;(c).other VOCs;(d).Totol biogenic VOCs", "references": ["As shown in Fig. 4, BVOC emissions in China are mostly centralized in the northeast and southeast and in southern Yunnan, Hainan and Taiwan Provinces. Specifically, the forest area in Yunnan accounts for nearly  $10\%$  of all forest cover in China. Therefore, Yunnan Province has the highest BVOC emissions, and the large area of tropical rainforest cover in southern Yunnan leads to substantially higher BVOC emissions in the southern region than in other regions. The high emissions in the", "Biogenic volatile organic compounds (BVOCs) emitted from terrestrial ecosystems have substantial effects on the global climate and environmental chemistry. Previous studies have shown that up to  $90\%$  of the total VOC emissions are derived from biogenic sources (Guenther et al. 1995). BVOCs such as isoprene, monoterpenes and sesquiterpenes participate in oxidative chemical reactions in the atmosphere with oxidants such as ozone, OH and  $\mathrm{NO}_3$  radicals. In addition, BVOCs are major sources of secondary organic aerosols (SOA) and new particle formation (Hallquist et al. 2009; Kulmala et al. 2004; Paasonen et al. 2013; Kota et al. 2015). Measurements and modeling of BVOC emissions are of vital importance to understand the carbon cycle, biosphere-atmosphere interactions and climate change (Gu et al. 2017; Hantson et al. 2017).", "Table 3 illustrates the estimated annual emissions of BVOCs in this work and other studies. The estimate of annual BVOC emissions was  $23.54\mathrm{Tg}$ , which is within the range reported in previous estimates, ranging from  $12.83\mathrm{Tg}$  to  $42.5\mathrm{Tg}$  between 1990 and 2006. Estimates in the present work are greater than the  $20.6\mathrm{Tg}$  for 2000 estimated by Klinger et al. (2002) and  $12.83\mathrm{Tg}$  for 2003 estimated by Chi and Xie (2011). However, the estimates in the present study are lower than the results of Guenther et al. (1995) and Li et al. (2013), who obtained values of  $28.4\mathrm{Tg}$  for 1990 and  $42.5\mathrm{Tg}$  for 2003, respectively. Differences among the discussed studies can be attributed to various factors. It is reported that the forest coverage, the percentage of which increased from  $16.6\%$  in 2003 to  $21.63\%$  in 2014, and the forest area increased from  $1.59\times 10^{8}\mathrm{hm}^{2}$  in 2003 to  $2.08\times 10^{8}\mathrm{hm}^{2}$  in 2014 (China Forestry Bureau (CFB), 2014), which may be the main reasons for large discrepancies. In addition, BVOC emissions were largely followed by LAI changes (Souri et al. 2017). Chen et al. (2019) utilized satellite data to analyze the annual average MODIS LAI in China from 2000 to 2017 and observed a significant increasing trend in eastern China (the trend was higher than  $18\times 10^{-2}\mathrm{m}^2$  per  $\mathrm{m}^2$  per decade; see Fig. S3 in the Supporting Information). Therefore, the interannual variation of LAI is also an important factor which cause the differences. Besides, some of the discrepancies can also be explained by the use of different algorithms and emission factors. In this study, the MEGAN default global average EFs were applied to estimate BVOC emissions. Li et al. (2012) used a simplified isoprene algorithm (PCEEA) and neglected the effect of soil moisture and detailed canopy information, which resulted in a much lower estimate of  $12.97\mathrm{Tg}$  in 2006 than our study. Yu and Hong (2012) adopted lower EFs and leaf biomass densities for each plant type derived from a small number of local measurements, which caused an underestimation of emissions. There is a large difference of isoprene emission", "northeast are due to the high forest coverage of the genus and eucalyptus forest which have the highest isoprene emission potential. Taiwan's high emissions originate from evergreen broadleaf forests. The distribution of various deciduous, hardwood and mixed broadleaf trees account for the high emissions in southeastern China and the Qinglin Mountains. Owing to the wide coverage of tropical rainforests, the BVOC emissions in Hainan Province are consequently high. Because of the principal contribution of isoprene to total BVOCs, the spatial distribution of total BVOC emissions is similar to that of forest distribution. Terpene emissions are concentrated in the southern part of China (especially in the southeast) because of the high-density coniferous forests there. In southwestern China, especially in the Sichuan-Tibet region, high altitudes and low temperatures on the Tibetan Plateau have led to low levels of emissions in the region. In the Sichuan Basin (SCB), although the forest and shrub coverages are high, isoprene emissions are relatively low. This trend may be caused by the low temperatures in western Sichuan and the low solar radiation in eastern Sichuan, coupled with the closed terrain and extensive cloud cover in the atmosphere Lin and Li, 2016). In addition, high OVOC emissions lead to high total BVOC emissions in most parts of southern China.", "H., Hoffmann, T., Iinuma, Y., Jang, M., Jenkin, M.E., Jimenez, J.L., Kiendler-Scharr, A., Maenhaut, W., McFiggans, G., Mentel, T.F., Monod, A., Prévot, A.S.H., Seinfeld, J.H., Surratt, J.D., Szmigielski, R., Wildt, J., 2009. The formation, properties and impact of secondary organic aerosol: current and emerging issues. Atmos. Chem. Phys. 9, 5155-5236. https://doi.org/10.5194/acp-9-5155-2009.

content.title: Fig. 1. Results of the BAB II quality assurance measures for  8NO / NO_x  instruments. Readings of the 8 instruments measuring ambient air with their gas inlet systems located very close to each other (y-axis) are compared with the mean value of all instruments (x-axis) before (left diagram, raw data) and after data correction (right diagram, corrected data).
content.summary: The figure compares NO_x instrument readings before and after applying quality assurance measures, highlighting improved data alignment post-correction.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates the effectiveness of quality assurance measures in harmonizing NO_x monitor readings, reducing deviations significantly post-correction.

content.title: Fig. 7. Change in ambient concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons of 2014-2019.
content.summary: Fig. 7 shows the change in ambient concentrations of OVOCs from various sources during the dry seasons of 2014-2019, highlighting decreasing contributions from primary anthropogenic sources.
content.insight: The data demonstrates significant reductions in primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs due to stringent pollution control measures, leading to a noticeable decline in overall ambient concentrations during the study period.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_153", "source_md": "Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga\\figure_7.jpg", "title": "Fig. 7. Change in ambient concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons of 2014-2019.", "references": ["Fig. 7 presents the changes in absolute concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons for the six-year study period. To better depict the contribution trends of different OVOC sources, the parameters were normalized (based on", "Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.", "Previous studies have proven that some low-carbon OVOCs (formaldehyde and acetaldehyde) can generate OH radicals and hydroperoxyl radicals  $\\mathrm{(HO_2)}$  through photolysis to affect the oxidizing capacity of the atmosphere (Volkamer et al., 2010; Shao et al., 2011; Xue et al., 2016). OVOCs can also participate in photochemical reactions and contribute to the generation of  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  and SOA (Louie et al., 2013; Ehn et al., 2014). In addition, as most OVOCs are highly water soluble, they can be absorbed into the human body, irritating the eyes, skin and respiratory tract, thus harming human health. OVOCs such as formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, acetone and acrolein have been shown to pose serious health risks (WHO, 2000, Delfino et al., 2003; Bashkin, 2009; Du et al., 2014). Given the important role of OVOCs in photochemical reactions and the complexity of their sources, OVOCs have been the subject of extensive research recently (Sahu et al., 2017; Maji et al., 2020), especially in China, where air pollution is severe (Zhang et al., 2012, 2015; Zhou et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Zhang et al. (2012) studied the pollution characteristics of BTEX (benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, m,p-xylene and o-xylene) and carbonyl compounds during summer in Beijing and found that the  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  formation potential of carbonyl compounds  $(166.1~\\mu \\mathrm{g} / \\mathrm{m}^3)$  was approximately 2.5 times that of BTEX  $(65.4~\\mu \\mathrm{g} / \\mathrm{m}^3)$ . Zhou et al. (2019) used proton transfer reaction mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) to measure VOCs in Xi'an and used the positive matrix factorization (PMF) model to analyze over 30 types of VOCs and identify eight primary sources and found that local emissions, such as industrial processes, solvent usage, residential biofuel use and vehicular exhaust, contributed about  $70\\%$  of the VOCs measured. Zhu et al. (2019) used PTR-MS to monitor OVOCs during different seasons and applied photochemical age parameter analysis to evaluate the sources of OVOCs in Shenzhen. They found that primary anthropogenic sources were the most important source of OVOCs in urban areas of China.", "Shenzhen belongs to the subtropical marine monsoon climate zone. Marked differences in the four seasons were not evident, and the observation time each year was slightly different; therefore, this study divided the entire year into a dry season and a wet season according to the monthly temperature and precipitation. As presented in Figure S1, the period from May to October, with higher temperatures and abundant precipitation, was defined as the wet season, and the period from November to April, with less precipitation, was defined as the dry season. The meteorological data are from the Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality (Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). In addition to the differences in temperature and precipitation, the sources of air masses in Shenzhen during the wet and dry seasons were also very different. Taking January and July 2018 as an example, the 48-h backward trajectories at  $100\\mathrm{m}$  above ground level in Shenzhen  $(113.9^{\\circ}\\mathrm{E}, 22.6^{\\circ}\\mathrm{N})$  were calculated beginning at 12:00 UTC using the Hybrid Single Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model (Draxler and Rolph, 2003). The results show that the origin of the air mass passing through Shenzhen in different seasons was very different: during the dry season, the air mass mainly came from the northern mainland, and during the wet season, it mainly came from the South China Sea, which has also been explained in detail in our previous studies (Zou et al., 2017). In addition, referencing existing studies of air pollution in the Pearl River Delta, many scholars have adopted a method of dividing the wet and dry seasons (Cui et al., 2015). Figure S2 shows the changes in major meteorological parameters during the six-year observation period in Shenzhen. Analysis of major meteorological parameters in Shenzhen (Fig S2) did not indicate significant interannual variability in temperature, rainfall, humidity or wind conditions that could have substantially influenced the long-term variation in atmospheric pollutants observed in this study.", "PRD and to improve the accuracy of the source apportionment. First, we chose benzene as the tracer of primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs. Generally, CO (de Gouw et al., 2005, 2018), acetylene (Yuan et al., 2012) and benzene (Ma et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019) are commonly used tracers representing anthropogenic sources of VOCs. Anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in China are rather complicated, with industrial solvents and motor vehicle exhaust contributing significantly to VOCs. However, CO and acetylene mainly come from combustion emissions, which cannot represent well the anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in China. Compared with CO and acetylene, benzene can better reflect the anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in the PRD. For example, Zhang et al. (2013) used the PMF model to analyze the source of VOCs in the PRD and found that combustion processes and industrial emissions were the predominant sources of benzene. Second, considering the photolysis of aldehydes and ketones during the daytime, which obviously conflicts with assumption (2), we need to consider the effect of the photodegradation of aldehydes and ketones on their removal. Referring to the studies of Wang et al. (2017) and Huang et al. (2020), we introduced a coefficient  $(\\mathrm{m})$  before the OH reaction rate constant  $\\left(\\mathrm{k}_{\\mathrm{ovoc}}\\right)$  to compensate for the daytime loss of aldehydes and ketones caused by photolysis. A detailed description of the calculation method of the m value is given in the supporting information. For methanol, formic acid and acetic acid, with almost no photolysis (Saunders et al., 2003), this study ignored the effect of photolysis on daytime removal (set m as 1), while the calculated m values for acetaldehyde, acetone and methyl ethyl ketone (MEK) were 1.02, 1.26 and 1.25, respectively, during the sampling period. Third, previous studies have shown that biomass burning emissions are an inevitable source of OVOCs (Yuan et al., 2010; Warneke et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2016). For example, Yuan et al. (2010) reported that biomass burning emissions in the PRD contributed  $9.5 - 17.7\\%$  to nine kinds of VOCs (including methanol, acetone, and MEK), suggesting that the contribution of biomass burning to OVOCs cannot be ignored. Therefore, the last parameter  $(ER_{bb} * [\\text{Tracer}_{bb}])$  in Equation (1) was added in this study to estimate the contribution of biomass burning to OVOCs.  $[\\text{Tracer}_{bb}]$  represents the concentration of ambient acetonitrile, which is an excellent indicator for biomass burning and can be accurately measured by PTR-MS (de Gouw et al., 2003b). In addition, previous studies have shown that benzene mainly comes from transportation and solvent use in the PRD, and biomass burning accounts for less than  $10\\%$  (Lyu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2013), so the influence of using benzene as a tracer of anthropogenic sources on biomass burning emissions can be ignored.  $\\mathrm{ER}_{bb}$  is the emission factor of OVOCs relative to acetonitrile, which was calculated by least-squares fit along with  $\\mathrm{ER}_{\\mathrm{OVOC}}$ ,  $\\mathrm{ER}_{\\mathrm{precursor}}$ ,  $\\mathrm{ER}_{\\mathrm{biogen}}$ ,  $\\mathrm{k}_{\\mathrm{precursor}}$ , and [background].", "It should be emphasized that because the sampling data of this study span six years, in addition to the above assumptions, one more assumption should be added: (4) during the sampling period, there was no significant change in the ratio of OVOC anthropogenic emissions to tracers (benzene) in Shenzhen. The emission reduction strategy of Shenzhen is to reduce emission sources of pollutants as a whole, such as replacing gasoline vehicles with electric vehicles and closing down or moving VOC-emitting enterprises to other areas. These measures have reduced emissions of VOCs, but the emission ratio among specific VOC species is relatively stable. Toluene/benzene (T/B) was considered a good indicator to judge the contribution of motor vehicle sources and solvent use to VOC emissions (Nelson and Quigley, 1984; Wang et al., 2002; Araizaga et al., 2013). Figure S5 shows the changes in the concentrations of benzene and toluene and T/B from 2014 to 2019. The concentrations of benzene and toluene in the six years showed a downward trend, while T/B remained at approximately 5, which also indicated", "A total of 17 species of VOCs were monitored in this study by PTR-MS from 2014 to 2019, including 6 species classified as OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, formic acid, acetic acid, and MEK); 5 aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, styrene, C8 and C9 aromatics); 3 classified as biogenic species (isoprene, MVK + MACR, and monoterpenes); and 2 species that, in addition to benzene, were measured as tracers (acetonitrile and DMS). The compounds, the respective  $\\mathrm{m} / \\mathrm{z}$  of their parent ion signal, and their detection limits are provided in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2, the average of the sum of the VOC concentrations in the dry season over the six years ranged from 25.3 to 41.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 33.1 ppbv; for the wet season, the average value ranged from 23.0 to 39.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 29.4 ppbv. In terms of the proportion of each component, in the six years of dry and wet seasons, OVOCs (70–78%) accounted for the largest proportion, followed by aromatic hydrocarbons (16–26%), whereas the concentrations of biogenic VOCs (BVOCs) and tracers were relatively low (less than 10%). Figure S6 shows the time series of 17 species of VOCs during the sampling period. To evaluate the concentrations of OVOCs in this study, comparative results of the concentrations of six OVOCs in five urban areas in China are listed in Table 1. Compared to Guangzhou and Hong Kong, two other mega-cities in the PRD region, the concentrations of acetaldehyde, acetone, and MEK in Shenzhen were higher than those in Hong", "Fig. 2. Concentrations of VOC components during dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.", "The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\\%$ ). In contrast to other OVOCs, acetone was the most affected by regional background concentration  $(37\\%)$  which may be because acetone is relatively inactive and widely exists in the atmosphere, and its ground-level background mixing ratios can reach 500 pptv throughout the troposphere under extremely clean conditions (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016). For formic acid, secondary anthropogenic sources contributed the most  $(34\\%)$", "Fig. 6 shows the trends in the relative contributions to OVOCs from 2014 to 2019 during the dry and wet seasons. In general, during the six years from 2014 to 2019, primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to OVOCs during Shenzhen's dry season, but the proportion exhibited a decreasing trend year over year, and the proportion during the six-year period decreased by approximately  $15\\%$  overall. This continuous decreasing trend indicates that the VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas, where most of the industry and traffic of Shenzhen is located, had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. The OVOC source compositions during the wet season, when clean marine air masses from the south dominated the sampling site, did not exhibit a clear year-by-year trend. The contributions of secondary anthropogenic sources to OVOC concentrations (except that of methanol) during the wet season were significantly higher (approximately 1.3-1.6 times greater) than those during the dry season, indicating that secondary transformation of NMHCs during the wet season contributed greatly to OVOC concentrations. Biogenic sources reasonably contributed a larger proportion of OVOC concentrations during the wet season, whereas biomass burning contributed a larger fraction of acetaldehyde during the dry season.", "the OVOC concentrations in 2014). From 2014 to 2019, the ambient concentration of OVOCs contributed by primary anthropogenic sources decreased significantly, declining by  $50 - 60\\%$ . Among the six OVOC species, the species with the largest decrease in concentration attributed to primary anthropogenic sources was methanol, which decreased by 7 ppb. However, contributions of the other sources to the absolute concentrations of OVOCs did not significantly change over the six-year period. Specifically, annual variations in secondary anthropogenic sources were not significant, indicating that secondary formation of atmospheric OVOCs in this region might be restricted by the atmospheric oxidizing capacity and that precursor emissions are more than abundant.", "hydrocarbon and 490 tons of NOx. VOC emission reduction in industrial enterprises and the coating industry occurs mainly through upgrading emission standards and closing down industries with high VOC emissions. For example, furniture manufacturing used to be an important industry in Shenzhen but also a key VOC-emitting industry. From 2010 to 2016, 565 coating production lines were removed or relocated in Shenzhen, reducing VOC emissions by more than 30,000 tons every year. Since 2017, Shenzhen has completely banned solvent paints and adhesives in the manufacturing process and replaced them with water-based paints to further reduce VOC emissions, especially the emissions of aldehydes and ketones commonly used in the production of furniture manufacturing (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). As a commonly used tracer of anthropogenic sources of VOCs, the annual average concentration of benzene during the dry season from 2014 to 2019 was 0.98, 0.81, 0.64, 0.61, 0.49, and 0.45 ppb (Figure S5), respectively, reflecting the decreasing trend in primary VOC emissions in recent years. Therefore, the decrease in OVOC concentrations during the dry season over the six-year study period could primarily be attributed to decreases in the contributions from primary anthropogenic sources. However, the contribution of secondary anthropogenic", "PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.", "sources are responsible for the volatile organic compounds in the atmosphere of Pearl River Delta. content.title: Fig. 6. Six-year trends of the contributions from different sources to daytime OVOC concentrations.
content.summary: The figure displays six-year trends in contributions from various sources to daytime OVOC concentrations in Shenzhen during dry and wet seasons.
content.insight: The decreasing trend in primary anthropogenic sources indicates effective VOC control measures, while secondary sources show less variability, highlighting the complexity of managing OVOC levels.

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_152", "source_md": "Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Fig. 6. Six-year trends of the contributions from different sources to daytime OVOC concentrations.", "references": ["Fig. 6 shows the trends in the relative contributions to OVOCs from 2014 to 2019 during the dry and wet seasons. In general, during the six years from 2014 to 2019, primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to OVOCs during Shenzhen's dry season, but the proportion exhibited a decreasing trend year over year, and the proportion during the six-year period decreased by approximately  $15\%$  overall. This continuous decreasing trend indicates that the VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas, where most of the industry and traffic of Shenzhen is located, had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. The OVOC source compositions during the wet season, when clean marine air masses from the south dominated the sampling site, did not exhibit a clear year-by-year trend. The contributions of secondary anthropogenic sources to OVOC concentrations (except that of methanol) during the wet season were significantly higher (approximately 1.3-1.6 times greater) than those during the dry season, indicating that secondary transformation of NMHCs during the wet season contributed greatly to OVOC concentrations. Biogenic sources reasonably contributed a larger proportion of OVOC concentrations during the wet season, whereas biomass burning contributed a larger fraction of acetaldehyde during the dry season.", "Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.", "As presented in Fig. 1, Shenzhen is located in the PRD of Guangdong Province, China. The sampling site for this study was located on the campus of the Shenzhen Graduate School of Peking University (PKUSZ,  $22.60^{\circ}\mathrm{N}$ ,  $113.97^{\circ}\mathrm{E}$ ) in western Shenzhen, which is predominantly surrounded by green areas, teaching buildings and apartments. No evident anthropogenic emission sources of air pollutants surrounded the sampling site, except a highway approximately  $100\mathrm{m}$  away. Previous studies have shown that the concentrations of air pollutants such as  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and VOCs at this site were approximately equal to their average concentrations in the entire PRD region (Louie et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2018). Therefore, this site was considered suitable for long-term observation of trends in air pollutants in the PRD region. Table S1 summarizes the effective days of VOCs observed in each month from 2014 to 2019, with 1169 days in total over the six years of sampling. During the six-year sampling period, the loss of data was mainly due to instrument failure and bad weather, especially in the wet season due to frequent rainfall, resulting in a higher rate of missing data during", "Shenzhen belongs to the subtropical marine monsoon climate zone. Marked differences in the four seasons were not evident, and the observation time each year was slightly different; therefore, this study divided the entire year into a dry season and a wet season according to the monthly temperature and precipitation. As presented in Figure S1, the period from May to October, with higher temperatures and abundant precipitation, was defined as the wet season, and the period from November to April, with less precipitation, was defined as the dry season. The meteorological data are from the Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality (Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). In addition to the differences in temperature and precipitation, the sources of air masses in Shenzhen during the wet and dry seasons were also very different. Taking January and July 2018 as an example, the 48-h backward trajectories at  $100\mathrm{m}$  above ground level in Shenzhen  $(113.9^{\circ}\mathrm{E}, 22.6^{\circ}\mathrm{N})$  were calculated beginning at 12:00 UTC using the Hybrid Single Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model (Draxler and Rolph, 2003). The results show that the origin of the air mass passing through Shenzhen in different seasons was very different: during the dry season, the air mass mainly came from the northern mainland, and during the wet season, it mainly came from the South China Sea, which has also been explained in detail in our previous studies (Zou et al., 2017). In addition, referencing existing studies of air pollution in the Pearl River Delta, many scholars have adopted a method of dividing the wet and dry seasons (Cui et al., 2015). Figure S2 shows the changes in major meteorological parameters during the six-year observation period in Shenzhen. Analysis of major meteorological parameters in Shenzhen (Fig S2) did not indicate significant interannual variability in temperature, rainfall, humidity or wind conditions that could have substantially influenced the long-term variation in atmospheric pollutants observed in this study.", "It should be emphasized that because the sampling data of this study span six years, in addition to the above assumptions, one more assumption should be added: (4) during the sampling period, there was no significant change in the ratio of OVOC anthropogenic emissions to tracers (benzene) in Shenzhen. The emission reduction strategy of Shenzhen is to reduce emission sources of pollutants as a whole, such as replacing gasoline vehicles with electric vehicles and closing down or moving VOC-emitting enterprises to other areas. These measures have reduced emissions of VOCs, but the emission ratio among specific VOC species is relatively stable. Toluene/benzene (T/B) was considered a good indicator to judge the contribution of motor vehicle sources and solvent use to VOC emissions (Nelson and Quigley, 1984; Wang et al., 2002; Araizaga et al., 2013). Figure S5 shows the changes in the concentrations of benzene and toluene and T/B from 2014 to 2019. However, the six OVOCs exhibited a significant upward trend during the daytime. These results suggested that photochemical production and/or daytime anthropogenic activities related to OVC emissions were notable and could overwhelm photochemical loss. (Guo et al., 2011, Zheng et al., 2013). The diurnal variations in VOCs in our previous two individual campaigns, which were carried out during the dry season (Huang et al., 2019, 2020), were almost the same as those in the dry season in this study.", "The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). Since 2017, Shenzhen has completely banned solvent paints and adhesives in the manufacturing process and replaced them with water-based paints to further reduce VOC emissions, especially the emissions of aldehydes and ketones commonly used in the production of furniture manufacturing (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). As a commonly used tracer of anthropogenic sources of VOCs, the annual average concentration of benzene during the dry season from 2014 to 2019 was 0.98, 0.81, 0.64, 0.61, 0.49, and 0.45 ppb (Figure S5), respectively, reflecting the decreasing trend in primary VOC emissions in recent years. The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\mathrm{ppb}$ . The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.", "j.envpol.2019.113651.

content.title: Fig. 2. Concentrations of VOC components during dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.
content.summary: Figure 2 displays the concentrations of VOC components in dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019, highlighting the dominance of OVOCs and the seasonal variations in VOC proportions.
content.insight: The figure illustrates the significant seasonal variation in VOC concentrations with OVOCs being the most prevalent, underscoring their role in atmospheric chemistry and the impact of pollution control measures.
